items.title: Prevent Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
items.rule: Ensure that server-side requests are properly validated to prevent SSRF attacks.
items.whyIsThisImportant: SSRF vulnerabilities allow attackers to manipulate server-side requests, potentially accessing internal resources or exposing sensitive data.

items.title: Ensure Secure Deserialization
items.rule: Ensure that deserialization processes validate untrusted data before processing to prevent code execution vulnerabilities.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Deserializing untrusted data without validation can allow attackers to inject malicious objects, leading to remote code execution and security breaches.

items.title: Use Appropriate HTTP Status Codes
items.rule: Ensure that HTTP handlers return the appropriate status codes based on request success or failure.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Returning incorrect HTTP status codes can lead to ambiguous API behavior, making it harder for clients to handle responses correctly.

items.title: Ensure `@PathVariable` is Used for Path Variables
items.rule: Ensure that `@PathVariable` is used correctly in Spring applications to bind URI path variables to method parameters.
items.whyIsThisImportant: In Spring, failing to use `@PathVariable` with a path template placeholder (`/api/resource/{id}`) can cause unexpected behavior or missing parameter errors.

items.title: Avoid Using `@Qualifier` on `@Bean` Methods
items.rule: Ensure that `@Qualifier` is not used on `@Bean` methods, as explicit bean titles should be used instead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using `@Qualifier` on `@Bean` methods is redundant and can cause unnecessary complexity in Spring applications. Beans should be titled explicitly using the method title or the `title` attribute.

items.title: Avoid ClassCastException Due to Unsafe Casting
items.rule: Ensure that `instanceof` checks are used before performing explicit type casting to prevent `ClassCastException`.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Unsafe casting can lead to runtime `ClassCastException` errors. Always use `instanceof` checks before performing type casting.

items.title: Always Verify Server Certificates in SSL/TLS Connections
items.rule: Ensure that SSL/TLS certificate validation is always enabled to prevent Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Disabling SSL/TLS certificate validation exposes applications to Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks, allowing attackers to intercept and manipulate sensitive data.

items.title: Retrieve Only Necessary Fields in SQL Queries
items.rule: Ensure that SQL queries specify only the necessary fields instead of using `SELECT *` to optimize database performance.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using `SELECT *` retrieves more data than necessary, increasing database load, network traffic, and memory usage. Query only required fields to optimize performance.

items.title: Keep Lambdas Concise
items.rule: Ensure that lambda expressions remain short and simple. If a lambda grows too complex, refactor it into a titled method.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Lambdas should remain short and simple. Large, complex lambdas reduce readability and should be refactored into titled methods for better maintainability.

items.title: Use Batch Processing in JDBC
items.rule: Ensure that batch processing is used instead of executing individual SQL statements inside loops for better database efficiency.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Executing individual SQL statements in a loop is inefficient. Using batch processing improves performance by reducing database communication overhead.

items.title: Use Object Types for Optional REST Parameters
items.rule: Ensure that optional REST parameters use object types or `Optional<T>` instead of primitive types to avoid runtime errors.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using primitive types for optional REST parameters can lead to runtime errors when the parameter is absent. Use object types or `Optional<T>` instead.

items.title: Prefer Constructor Injection for Spring Components
items.rule: Ensure that constructor injection is used instead of field injection (`@Autowired` on fields) in Spring components for better testability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using field injection (`@Autowired` on fields) in Spring components increases coupling and testability issues. Constructor injection is more reliable and preferred.

items.title: Avoid Brain Methods (Methods Doing Too Many Tasks)
items.rule: Ensure that methods perform a single, well-defined task. Large methods should be refactored into smaller, focused methods.
items.whyIsThisImportant: A method that performs too many operations becomes difficult to understand, maintain, and reuse. Large methods should be broken down into smaller, focused methods.

items.title: Avoid Monster Classes (Excessive Dependencies)
items.rule: Ensure that large classes with multiple responsibilities are refactored into smaller, more manageable classes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Classes with too many responsibilities become difficult to maintain and violate the Single Responsibility Principle. Refactor to separate concerns into smaller, independent classes.

items.title: Use Java Records for Immutable Data Structures
items.rule: Ensure that Java records are used for immutable data structures instead of manually implementing immutable classes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Java records provide built-in immutability, reducing the risk of accidental modifications. Avoid manually creating immutable classes when records can be used.

items.title: Avoid Redundant Null Checks with `instanceof`
items.rule: Ensure that explicit null checks are not used with `instanceof`, as `null` is never an instance of any class.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `null` is never an instance of any class, making explicit null checks redundant when using `instanceof`. This check should be removed.

examples.snippet:
if (x != null && x instanceof MyClass) { ... }  // Noncompliant

if (x == null || ! x instanceof MyClass) { ... } // Noncompliant

if (x instanceof MyClass) { ... }

if (! x instanceof MyClass) { ... }

items.title: Use `var` Wisely
items.rule: Check if `var` is used only when the variable type is clear and enhances readability. Avoid using `var` when the type is not obvious.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Overusing `var` can make code harder to read, especially when the variable type isn't obvious. Use it only when it enhances readability.

examples.snippet: int x = 42; // Explicit type improves readability.

items.title: Prefer Named Classes Over Tuples
items.rule: Ensure that titled classes are used instead of untitled tuples for complex data structures. Tuples should only be used when their purpose is immediately clear.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Tuples without descriptive titles can make code difficult to understand and maintain. Named classes provide clarity and improve code readability.

examples.snippet: Tuple<int, string> result = new Tuple<int, string>(1, "Hello");

items.title: Remove Items Safely During Iteration
items.rule: Verify that collections are not modified directly during iteration. Instead, ensure safe removal techniques such as iterating over a copy or using `ToList()`.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Removing items directly from a collection while iterating can cause runtime exceptions. Use safe removal techniques such as `ToList()`.

items.title: Avoid Excessively Deep Namespaces
items.rule: Ensure that titlespace structures are not overly deep. Favor a flat, organized structure to enhance readability and navigation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Deeply nested titlespaces increase code complexity, making navigation and maintenance more difficult.

items.title: Organize Class Members by Accessibility
items.rule: Check if class members are grouped by accessibility (`public`, `protected`, `private`) to improve code readability and maintainability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Poorly organized class members make code harder to navigate and maintain. Grouping members by accessibility improves readability.

items.title: Use `readonly` or `const` for Immutable Data
items.rule: Ensure that immutable instance fields are marked as `readonly`, and compile-time constants are marked as `const`.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Mutable fields can lead to unintended modifications. Use `readonly` for instance fields and `const` for compile-time constants.

items.title: Limit Lengthy LINQ Chains
items.rule: Verify that LINQ queries are not excessively long. Break complex queries into smaller, more understandable expressions.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Overly long LINQ queries reduce readability and make debugging difficult. Break them into smaller, more understandable expressions.

examples.snippet: var result = someList.Where(x => x.IsValid).OrderBy(x => x.Name).Select(x => x.Value).ToList();

items.title: Ensure Consistent Attribute Placement
items.rule: Check if attributes are consistently placed in the code, maintaining uniform formatting across the project.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Inconsistent placement of attributes can cause confusion and make code harder to read and modify.

items.title: Use Expression-Bodied Members Where Appropriate
items.rule: Ensure that simple methods and properties use expression-bodied syntax to improve code conciseness and readability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Expression-bodied members improve code conciseness and readability for simple properties and methods.

items.title: Ensure Parameter Names Match Base Methods
items.rule: Verify that method parameters in overrides and interface implementations match the base method’s parameter titles to maintain consistency.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Changing parameter titles when implementing an interface or overriding a base method reduces readability and can confuse developers.

items.title: Avoid Using `goto`
items.rule: Check if `goto` statements are avoided. Ensure that structured control flow statements (`if`, `for`, `while`) are used instead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `goto` leads to unstructured, difficult-to-maintain code. Use structured control flow statements (`if`, `for`, `while`, etc.) instead.

items.title: Use `First`/`Single` Instead of `FirstOrDefault`/`SingleOrDefault` for Non-Empty Collections
items.rule: Ensure `First()` or `Single()` is used instead of `FirstOrDefault()` or `SingleOrDefault()` when collections are guaranteed to have elements.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `FirstOrDefault` and `SingleOrDefault` imply that a collection might be empty, leading to unnecessary null checks and potential bugs.

examples.snippet:
var items = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };

int firstItem = items.FirstOrDefault(); // Noncompliant, this implies the collection might be empty, when we know it is not

var items = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };

int firstItem = items.First(); // Compliant

items.title: API Controllers Should Inherit from `ControllerBase`
items.rule: Verify that API controllers inherit from `ControllerBase` instead of `Controller` unless views are explicitly required.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `ControllerBase` is the proper base class for API controllers, avoiding unnecessary dependencies on view-related functionality.

items.title: Mark Unchanged Variables as `const`
items.rule: Ensure that variables that never change are marked as `const` to prevent accidental modification and enhance code clarity.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Variables that never change should be marked as `const` to prevent accidental modification and improve code clarity.

items.title: Avoid Empty Finalizers
items.rule: Check that finalizers are not left empty. If cleanup is needed, ensure proper resource disposal is implemented instead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Empty finalizers introduce unnecessary performance overhead. If cleanup is needed, implement proper resource disposal instead.

items.title: Value Types Should Implement `IEquatable<T>`
items.rule: Ensure that value types (structs) implement `IEquatable<T>` for efficient equality comparisons, avoiding boxing and reflection.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Structs without `IEquatable<T>` use boxing and reflection for equality checks, reducing performance.

items.title: Use Start Index Instead of `Substring`
items.rule: Verify that when extracting substrings, a start index is specified to avoid unnecessary memory allocations.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Searching for characters within a substring without a start index can lead to inefficiency and unnecessary memory usage.

items.title: Ensure `StringBuilder` Data Is Used
items.rule: Ensure that `StringBuilder` instances are converted to strings using `ToString()` before use to prevent memory wastage.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Creating a `StringBuilder` without calling `ToString()` results in unused objects, wasting memory and reducing performance.

items.title: Mark Non-Derived Private Classes as `sealed`
items.rule: Check if private or `file`-scoped classes that are never inherited are explicitly marked as `sealed` to indicate they are final.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Private or `file`-scoped classes that are never inherited should be explicitly marked as `sealed` to indicate they are final.

items.title: Avoid Blocking Calls to Async Methods
items.rule: Blocking async methods with `.Result` or `.Wait()` can cause deadlocks and prevent efficient asynchronous execution. Use `await` instead for proper async behavior.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Blocking async methods with `.Result` or `.Wait()` can cause deadlocks and prevent efficient asynchronous execution. Use `await` instead for proper async behavior.

items.title: Secure Connection Strings from Injection
items.rule: Allowing user input in connection strings can lead to injection attacks. Always validate and sanitize inputs before constructing connection strings.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Allowing user input in connection strings can lead to injection attacks. Always validate and sanitize inputs before constructing connection strings.

items.title: Prevent Deserialization Injection
items.rule: Deserializing untrusted data can allow attackers to execute arbitrary code. Always validate and sanitize serialized inputs before processing.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Deserializing untrusted data can allow attackers to execute arbitrary code. Always validate and sanitize serialized inputs before processing.

items.title: Prevent SQL Injection in Queries
items.rule: Using unsanitized user input in SQL queries can lead to SQL injection attacks. Use parameterized queries to protect against malicious inputs.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using unsanitized user input in SQL queries can lead to SQL injection attacks. Use parameterized queries to protect against malicious inputs.

items.title: Prevent Numeric Overflow in Calculations
items.rule: Numeric overflows occur when values exceed the data type limits, leading to incorrect calculations. Use checked arithmetic where necessary.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Numeric overflows occur when values exceed the data type limits, leading to incorrect calculations. Use checked arithmetic where necessary.

items.title: Use PascalCase for Methods and Properties
items.rule: Consistent naming conventions improve readability and maintainability. Method and property titles should follow PascalCase.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Consistent naming conventions improve readability and maintainability. Method and property titles should follow PascalCase.

items.title: Use PascalCase for Type Names
items.rule: Following PascalCase for type titles ensures consistency and readability across the codebase.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Following PascalCase for type titles ensures consistency and readability across the codebase.

items.title: Avoid Private-Only Constructors
items.rule: Classes with only private constructors cannot be instantiated outside their own scope, potentially making them dead code.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Classes with only private constructors cannot be instantiated outside their own scope, potentially making them dead code.

items.title: Avoid Returning Unused Values
items.rule: Methods returning values that are never used introduce unnecessary complexity. Remove such return values to improve clarity.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Methods returning values that are never used introduce unnecessary complexity. Remove such return values to improve clarity.

items.title: Verify Server Certificates in SSL/TLS
items.rule: Disabling SSL/TLS certificate validation exposes applications to Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks, compromising security.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Disabling SSL/TLS certificate validation exposes applications to Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks, compromising security.

items.title: Avoid `new Guid()` When Not Needed
items.rule: Using `new Guid()` instead of `Guid.Empty` or `Guid.NewGuid()` can be misleading and lead to unintended behavior.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using `new Guid()` instead of `Guid.Empty` or `Guid.NewGuid()` can be misleading and lead to unintended behavior.

items.title: Verify SSL/TLS Server Certificates
items.rule: Skipping certificate validation enables attackers to impersonate trusted entities and intercept secure communications.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Skipping certificate validation enables attackers to impersonate trusted entities and intercept secure communications.

items.title: Avoid Constructing System Commands from User Input
items.rule: Passing user input directly into system commands can lead to command injection vulnerabilities. Always sanitize inputs before execution.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Passing user input directly into system commands can lead to command injection vulnerabilities. Always sanitize inputs before execution.

items.title: Use `string.IsNullOrEmpty()` for String Checks
items.rule: `string.Equals()` for checking empty strings is inefficient. Use `string.IsNullOrEmpty()` instead for clarity and performance.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `string.Equals()` for checking empty strings is inefficient. Use `string.IsNullOrEmpty()` instead for clarity and performance.

examples.snippet:
title != null && title.Length > 0 // Compliant but more error prone
!string.IsNullOrEmpty(title)
string.IsNullOrEmpty(title)

items.title: Avoid `DateTime.Now` for Timing Operations
items.rule: `DateTime.Now` is affected by daylight savings and system clock adjustments. Use `Stopwatch` for more accurate timing.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `DateTime.Now` is affected by daylight savings and system clock adjustments. Use `Stopwatch` for more accurate timing.

items.title: Annotate REST API Actions with HTTP Verb Attributes
items.rule: Without explicit HTTP method attributes, API behavior can be unclear, leading to route conflicts and unexpected results.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Without explicit HTTP method attributes, API behavior can be unclear, leading to route conflicts and unexpected results.

items.title: Ensure Controllers Follow Single Responsibility Principle
items.rule: Controllers handling multiple concerns become harder to maintain and test. Split responsibilities into separate controllers.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Controllers handling multiple concerns become harder to maintain and test. Split responsibilities into separate controllers.

items.title: Always Validate `ModelState.IsValid` in Controllers
items.rule: Failing to check `ModelState.IsValid` before processing user input can result in invalid or unverified data being accepted.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Failing to check `ModelState.IsValid` before processing user input can result in invalid or unverified data being accepted.

items.title: Enforce Authentication for LDAP Connections
items.rule: Allowing anonymous LDAP connections exposes directory data to unauthorized users. Always require authentication.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Allowing anonymous LDAP connections exposes directory data to unauthorized users. Always require authentication.

examples.snippet:
DirectoryEntry myDirectoryEntry = new DirectoryEntry(myADSPath); // Compliant; default DirectoryEntry.AuthenticationType property value is "Secure" since .NET Framework 2.0

DirectoryEntry myDirectoryEntry = new DirectoryEntry(myADSPath, "u", "p", AuthenticationTypes.Secure);

items.title: Prevent Reflection Injection Attacks
items.rule: Reflection methods that process untrusted input can be exploited for remote code execution. Always validate and sanitize external input before using reflection.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Reflection methods that process untrusted input can be exploited for remote code execution. Always validate and sanitize external input before using reflection.

items.title: Prevent Code Injection in Dynamic Execution
items.rule: Allowing untrusted data to be executed dynamically can lead to arbitrary code execution and security vulnerabilities. Always validate input before execution.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Allowing untrusted data to be executed dynamically can lead to arbitrary code execution and security vulnerabilities. Always validate input before execution.

items.title: Secure OS Command Arguments
items.rule: If user input is used in OS command arguments, it can be manipulated to expand access or execute unintended commands. Ensure proper sanitization to prevent injection.
items.whyIsThisImportant: If user input is used in OS command arguments, it can be manipulated to expand access or execute unintended commands. Ensure proper sanitization to prevent injection.

items.title: CSRF Protection Should Always Be Enabled
items.rule: Disabling CSRF protection allows attackers to trick users into performing unintended actions, such as modifying account details or making unauthorized transactions.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Disabling CSRF protection allows attackers to trick users into performing unintended actions, such as modifying account details or making unauthorized transactions.

items.title: Use ProducesResponseTypeAttribute for API Actions
items.rule: Without ProducesResponseTypeAttribute, Swagger cannot infer the response type of an action returning IActionResult, making API documentation unclear and unreliable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Without ProducesResponseTypeAttribute, Swagger cannot infer the response type of an action returning IActionResult, making API documentation unclear and unreliable.

items.title: Prevent NoSQL Injection Attacks
items.rule: Unsanitized user input in NoSQL queries can allow attackers to manipulate database queries, leading to data leaks or unauthorized modifications.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Unsanitized user input in NoSQL queries can allow attackers to manipulate database queries, leading to data leaks or unauthorized modifications.

items.title: Avoid Returning Null in Non-Async Task Methods
items.rule: Returning null from a Task or Task<T> method causes a NullReferenceException if awaited. Use Task.CompletedTask or Task.FromResult<T>(null) instead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Returning null from a Task or Task<T> method causes a NullReferenceException if awaited. Use Task.CompletedTask or Task.FromResult<T>(null) instead.

items.title: Specify Timeout for Regular Expressions
items.rule: Without a timeout, regex processing on untrusted input can be exploited for Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks. Always define a timeout when using regex.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Without a timeout, regex processing on untrusted input can be exploited for Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks. Always define a timeout when using regex.

items.title: Do Not Start Route Templates with '/' in ASP.NET
items.rule: Starting a route template with '/' in ASP.NET makes it absolute, ignoring controller-level routes and potentially breaking expected routing behavior.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Starting a route template with '/' in ASP.NET makes it absolute, ignoring controller-level routes and potentially breaking expected routing behavior.

items.title: Utility Classes Should Not Have Public Constructors
items.rule: Utility classes are not meant to be instantiated. Mark them as static or provide a private constructor to prevent unintended instantiation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Utility classes are not meant to be instantiated. Mark them as static or provide a private constructor to prevent unintended instantiation.

items.title: Use Awaitable Methods in Async Code
items.rule: Calling synchronous methods inside async code can block execution and reduce performance. Use awaitable methods to ensure proper async execution.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Calling synchronous methods inside async code can block execution and reduce performance. Use awaitable methods to ensure proper async execution.

items.title: Ensure Getters and Setters Access the Correct Fields
items.rule: Copy-pasting code can lead to errors where getters or setters access the wrong fields. Always verify that properties interact with the expected fields.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Copy-pasting code can lead to errors where getters or setters access the wrong fields. Always verify that properties interact with the expected fields.

items.title: Ensure `case` Statements Have an `else` Clause
items.rule: Check if every `case` statement includes an `else` clause. If an `else` clause is missing, ensure that a comment explicitly states why it is unnecessary.
items.whyIsThisImportant: A `case` statement without an `else` clause may not handle all possible cases, leading to unexpected behavior. Always include an `else` clause or explicitly document why it is unnecessary.

examples.snippet:
case param
  when 1
    do_something()
  when 2
    do_something_else()
end

case param
  when 1
    do_something()
  when 2
    do_something_else()
  else
    handle_error('error_message')
end

items.title: Ensure `if...elsif` Constructs End with an `else` Clause
items.rule: Verify that all `if...elsif` constructs include a final `else` clause. If `else` is missing, ensure there is a comment explaining why all cases are covered.
items.whyIsThisImportant: When an `if` statement has multiple `elsif` branches, it should always end with an `else` clause. This ensures all possible cases are handled, preventing unintended behavior.

examples.snippet:
if x == 0
  doSomething
elsif x == 1
  doSomethingElse
end

if x == 0
  doSomething
elsif x == 1
  doSomethingElse
else
  raise 'An error has occured'
end

items.title: Place Statements on Separate Lines
items.rule: Ensure that multiple statements are not written on the same line. Each statement should be placed on a new line to improve readability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Writing multiple statements on a single line reduces readability and makes debugging harder. Separate statements should be placed on different lines for clarity.

items.title: Avoid Empty Methods
items.rule: Detect methods that have an empty body. If a method is empty, ensure that a comment is present explaining why it remains unimplemented.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Empty methods can be misleading, making it unclear whether they are unfinished, redundant, or intentionally blank. If an empty method is necessary, include a comment explaining why.

examples.snippet:
def shouldNotBeEmpty()  # Noncompliant - method is empty
end

def notImplemented()  # Noncompliant - method is empty
end

def emptyOnPurpose()  # Noncompliant - method is empty
end

items.title: Remove Unused Function Parameters
items.rule: Identify function parameters that are declared but never used within the function body. Remove them to improve clarity and maintainability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Declaring parameters that are never used in a function leads to confusion and unnecessary complexity. Remove unused parameters to improve code clarity and maintainability.

items.title: Do Not Leave Empty Code Blocks
items.rule: Check for empty code blocks (such as `if` or `loop` structures) that do not contain any statements. If an empty block is present, ensure it includes a comment explaining its purpose.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Empty code blocks make it unclear whether they are placeholders for future logic or mistakenly left blank. Always remove them or add a comment explaining their purpose.

examples.snippet:
def compute(a, b)
  sum = a + b
  if  sum > 0 # Noncompliant: is the block empty on purpose, or is code missing?
  end
  puts "Result: #{sum}"
end

items.title: Avoid Using Semicolons to Terminate Statements
items.rule: Detect unnecessary semicolons at the end of statements. Ruby does not require semicolons, so they should be removed unless multiple statements exist on the same line.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Ruby does not require semicolons at the end of statements. Using them unnecessarily reduces readability and goes against standard Ruby conventions.

items.title: Use Spaces Around Operators
items.rule: Ensure that arithmetic and logical operators have spaces around them for better readability (e.g., `x + y` instead of `x+y`).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Omitting spaces around operators (`+`, `-`, `*`, etc.) reduces code readability. Proper spacing makes expressions easier to read and understand.

items.title: Limit Line Length to 120 Characters
items.rule: Detect lines exceeding 120 characters and suggest breaking them into multiple lines for better readability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Keeping lines under 120 characters improves readability, especially on smaller screens or in side-by-side code comparisons.

examples.snippet:
def example
  long_line = "This is an example of a line that is way too long and exceeds the recommended limit of 120 characters."
end

def example
  long_line = "This is an example of a line that is way too long and " \
              "exceeds the recommended limit of 120 characters."
end

items.title: Follow `snake_case` for Method and Variable Names
items.rule: Verify that method and variable titles follow `snake_case` conventions, avoiding camelCase or PascalCase.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Ruby conventionally uses `snake_case` for method and variable titles. This improves consistency and makes code easier to read and maintain.

items.title: Use `CamelCase` for Class and Module Names
items.rule: Check if class and module titles use `CamelCase`. If not, suggest renaming them for consistency.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Classes and modules in Ruby should follow `CamelCase` naming conventions, making them easily distinguishable and improving code organization.

items.title: Avoid Unnecessary Defensive Programming
items.rule: Identify overly defensive code that checks for unrealistic conditions, such as excessive `nil?` checks or redundant type verifications.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Overly defensive programming that handles unrealistic scenarios increases code complexity. Focus on handling real-world edge cases instead.

examples.snippet:
def process(input)
  if input.nil? || !input.is_a?(String) || input.empty?
    return "Invalid input"
  end

  input.strip.upcase
end

def process(input)
  return unless input.is_a?(String) && !input.empty?

  input.strip.upcase
end

items.title: Prefer Guard Clauses Over Nested Conditionals
items.rule: Detect deeply nested conditionals and suggest using guard clauses (`return unless condition`) instead to improve readability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Deeply nested conditionals make code hard to follow. Using guard clauses (`return` early) improves clarity and reduces indentation depth.

examples.snippet:
def process(input)
  if input.is_a?(String)
    if !input.empty?
      return input.strip.upcase
    end
  end
  nil
end

items.title: Avoid Using Global Variables
items.rule: Check for global variable usage (e.g., variables prefixed with `$`). Suggest using instance variables, constants, or dependency injection instead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Global variables create hidden dependencies, making code harder to debug, maintain, and test. Use instance variables or constants instead.

examples.snippet:
class Counter
  def initialize
    @counter = 0
  end

  def increment
    @counter += 1
  end
end

items.title: Use Descriptive Exception Variable Names
items.rule: Ensure that exception variables in `rescue` blocks use meaningful titles instead of generic ones like `e`.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Naming exception variables generically (`e`) makes debugging harder. Use meaningful titles that describe the type or context of the error.

examples.snippet:
begin
  risky_operation
rescue => e
  puts e.message
end

items.title: Provide Meaningful Default Values for Hash Keys
items.rule: Check for hash key lookups without default values. If a missing key is accessed, suggest using `fetch` with a default value.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using meaningful default values prevents errors caused by uninitialized keys, improving code robustness and reducing unexpected `nil` values.

items.title: Avoid Rescuing the `Exception` Class
items.rule: Identify `rescue Exception` statements and suggest replacing them with `rescue StandardError` to avoid catching critical system exceptions.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Rescuing `Exception` captures critical system-level errors like `NoMemoryError`, preventing proper application termination and potentially masking serious issues.

examples.snippet:
begin
  risky_operation
rescue Exception
  puts "An error occurred"
end

begin
  risky_operation
rescue StandardError => error
  puts "An error occurred: #{error.message}"
end

items.title: Use `const` for Literal Constructor Parameters in `@immutable` Classes
items.rule: Ensure that all literal values passed to constructors of `@immutable` classes are marked as `const` to prevent unnecessary object creation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `@immutable` classes should have only `final` fields, and constructor parameters that are literals should be `const` to prevent accidental modifications.

items.title: `@immutable` Classes Should Only Have `const` Constructors
items.rule: Define constructors for `@immutable` classes as `const` to ensure that instances are created efficiently and cannot be modified.
items.whyIsThisImportant: An `@immutable` class should only have `const` constructors to enforce immutability and improve performance.

items.title: Avoid Unnecessary `toList()` with Spread Operator
items.rule: Remove unnecessary calls to `.toList()` when using the spread operator (`...`) as it is already designed to work with iterable collections.
items.whyIsThisImportant: The spread operators (`...` and `...?`) can flatten collections directly, making `toList()` redundant and less efficient.

items.title: Prefer `for` Elements Over `Map.fromIterable`
items.rule: Use `for` elements inside map literals instead of `Map.fromIterable` to improve readability and allow better compiler optimizations.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using `for` elements instead of `Map.fromIterable` improves readability and allows for compiler optimizations, making the code more performant.

items.title: Use `const` Instead of `static final` Where Possible
items.rule: Replace `static final` with `const` where applicable to enable compile-time evaluation and optimize performance.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `const` values are computed at compile-time, improving performance compared to `final`, which is computed at runtime.

items.title: Avoid Duplicating String Literals
items.rule: Store repeated string literals in constants or variables to improve maintainability and reduce redundancy.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Duplicated string literals make refactoring harder and increase the risk of inconsistencies in the codebase.

items.title: Utility Classes Should Not Have Public Constructors
items.rule: Make utility classes non-instantiable by providing a private constructor or marking them as `abstract`.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Utility classes contain only static members and should not be instantiated. Mark them as `abstract` or provide a private constructor.

items.title: Avoid Using Constant Patterns with Type Literals
items.rule: Do not use constant patterns with type literals as it can lead to unexpected behavior. Prefer direct type checking instead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using constant patterns with type literals can lead to incorrect assumptions and unintended behavior.

items.title: Always Use Curly Braces in Control Structures
items.rule: Use curly braces `{}` around all control structures (`if`, `for`, `while`, `do`-`while`) to improve readability and prevent logic errors.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Omitting curly braces in control structures can lead to errors when modifying the code. Always use `{}` for clarity.

items.title: Do Not Ignore Exceptions
items.rule: Always handle or log caught exceptions instead of silently ignoring them to improve debugging and maintain application stability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Ignoring exceptions can hide potential bugs. Handle them properly or log them for debugging purposes.

items.title: Avoid `return`, `break`, or `continue` in `finally` Blocks
items.rule: Do not use `return`, `break`, or `continue` inside `finally` blocks as they can suppress exceptions and lead to unintended behavior.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using control flow statements inside `finally` blocks suppresses exceptions and can lead to unexpected behavior.

items.title: Remove Unused Assignments
items.rule: Eliminate assignments to variables that are never used or overwritten immediately to improve code efficiency and readability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Assignments to variables that are never used waste computation and clutter the code. Remove them to improve clarity.

items.title: Prefer `SizedBox` Over `Container` for Spacing
items.rule: Use `SizedBox` instead of `Container` when adding spacing in layouts to improve performance and avoid unnecessary rendering overhead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `SizedBox` is a lightweight alternative to `Container` when only spacing is needed, reducing unnecessary widget complexity.

items.title: Check `mounted` Before Using `BuildContext` After an Async Operation
items.rule: Verify if a widget is still mounted before using `BuildContext` inside an asynchronous operation to prevent accessing invalid states.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `BuildContext` may become invalid if the widget is unmounted during an async operation. Always check `mounted` before using it.

items.title: Use Web-Only Libraries Only in Flutter Web Plugins
items.rule: Ensure `dart:html`, `dart:js`, and `dart:js_util` are only used in Flutter web plugins to prevent runtime errors in non-web environments.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `dart:html`, `dart:js`, and `dart:js_util` are only supported in web contexts. Using them outside of Flutter web plugins leads to runtime errors.

examples.snippet:
import 'dart:html';    // Non compliant
import 'dart:js';      // Non compliant
import 'dart:js_util'; // Non compliant
import 'package:other_package/other_library.dart';

items.title: Do Not Assign Values to `void` Variables
items.rule: Avoid assigning values to `void` variables as `void` is not a compatible type and doing so results in runtime errors.
items.whyIsThisImportant: In Dart, `void` is a type that does not hold values. Assigning a value to a `void` variable is a logical error.

examples.snippet:
dynamic x; // type of x not known
x = 3;

items.title: Avoid Unnecessary `toList()` with Spread Operator
items.rule: Remove unnecessary `.toList()` calls when using the spread operator (`...`) to avoid redundant operations.
items.whyIsThisImportant: The spread operator (`...` and `...?`) flattens collections directly, making `toList()` redundant and inefficient.

items.title: Prefer `is!` Over `!is` for Type Checks
items.rule: Use `is!` instead of `!is` for type checking to prevent confusion and ensure consistent behavior.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using `is!` instead of `!is` avoids confusion with null assertion syntax and improves code clarity.

items.title: Avoid Using `!` to Null Check Nullable Type Parameters
items.rule: Do not use `!` to check nullable type parameters, as this can lead to runtime exceptions if the type itself is nullable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: If a type parameter itself is nullable, using `!` for null checking can cause runtime exceptions. Use explicit checks instead.

items.title: Do Not Use Block Comments for Documentation
items.rule: Use `///` for documentation instead of block comments to improve tooling support and ensure better formatting in generated docs.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Block comments do not generate proper Dart documentation. Use `///` for documentation comments instead.

items.title: Use `///` Doc Comments for Members and Types
items.rule: Use `///` instead of regular comments to properly document Dart classes, methods, and properties for automatic documentation generation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: `///` comments allow tools like dartdoc to generate documentation, improving maintainability and usability.

examples.snippet:
// The number of characters in this chunk when unsplit.
int get length => ...

items.title: AVOID redundancy with the surrounding context
items.rule: Check if the method, variable, or class title contains redundant context (e.g., using 'carDrive()' when 'drive()' would suffice). Ensure the title is concise and avoids repetition of surrounding context.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Avoid redundant words in titles that repeat the surrounding context. For example, a class titled `Car` should not have a method `carDrive()`; instead, use `drive()`. Redundant naming decreases readability and clarity.

items.title: DO title extensions using UpperCamelCase
items.rule: Ensure that extensions use UpperCamelCase naming conventions (e.g., `MyExtension`). Avoid other casing styles that could reduce readability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Extensions should follow Dart's naming conventions, which use UpperCamelCase. Using other casing styles reduces readability and makes it inconsistent with other identifiers.

examples.snippet:
extension MyFancyList<T> on List<T> { ... }

extension SmartIterable<T> on Iterable<T> { ... }

items.title: DO title import prefixes using lowercase_with_underscores
items.rule: Check that import prefixes use lowercase_with_underscores (e.g., `my_library`), following Dart's style guide.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Import prefixes should use lowercase_with_underscores to improve readability and align with Dart's style guide. Using different naming conventions reduces clarity.

items.title: DO title other identifiers using lowerCamelCase
items.rule: Ensure that identifiers such as variables and method titles are written in lowerCamelCase (e.g., `myVariable`). Avoid using other naming conventions.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Identifiers (such as variable titles and method titles) should follow the lowerCamelCase naming convention. This improves consistency and readability.

items.title: PREFER using _, __, etc. for unused callback parameters
items.rule: Check for functions with unused parameters and ensure they are titled with `_` or `__` to indicate they are intentionally unused.
items.whyIsThisImportant: When defining a function with unused parameters, use `_` or `__` to indicate they are intentionally unused. This avoids confusion and prevents unnecessary warnings.

items.title: DON'T explicitly title libraries
items.rule: Ensure that libraries are not explicitly titled. Dart provides automatic library titles, so avoid redundant naming of libraries in the code.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Dart's library system provides automatic library titles based on file paths. Explicitly naming libraries is unnecessary and can lead to inconsistencies.

items.title: PREFER a noun phrase for a non-boolean property or variable
items.rule: Ensure that non-boolean properties or variables are titled with noun phrases (e.g., `userDetails`, not `getUserDetails`). This improves readability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Non-boolean properties and variables should be titled using noun phrases to improve clarity and readability. Using verb-like titles can cause confusion.

items.title: AVOID defining a class that contains only static members
items.rule: Ensure that classes with only static members are converted to top-level functions or extensions. Avoid unnecessary class instantiation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: If a class only contains static members, it should be converted into a top-level utility function or extension. This reduces unnecessary class instantiation and improves efficiency.

items.title: DO type annotate fields and top-level variables if the type isn't obvious
items.rule: Check that fields and top-level variables are type-annotated explicitly when the type is not obvious from the context. This avoids ambiguity.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using explicit type annotations improves readability and prevents ambiguity, especially when the type is not obvious from the assignment.

items.title: DON'T make the parameter to == nullable
items.rule: Ensure that parameters to `==` are non-nullable. Avoid using nullable parameters as they can introduce unexpected behavior and null reference errors.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Making the parameter to `==` nullable can introduce unexpected behavior. Always use a non-nullable parameter to prevent null reference errors.

items.title: Use Prepared (Parameterized) SQL Queries
items.rule: Do not build SQL queries by directly concatenating untrusted data. Instead, use prepared statements with parameters (for example, using PDO or MySQLi) to prevent SQL Injection.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Concatenating user input into SQL queries can allow an attacker to inject malicious commands into the database. Prepared statements ensure that data is treated as parameters, preventing unexpected SQL code execution.

items.title: Escape Output to Prevent XSS
items.rule: Always escape or sanitize user‐provided content before displaying it in HTML. Use functions like htmlspecialchars() to convert special characters into HTML entities.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Without escaping output, an attacker can inject malicious JavaScript (Cross‐Site Scripting) that will execute in other users’ browsers, compromising sensitive data and application integrity.

items.title: Avoid Using eval() to Execute Code
items.rule: Do not use the eval() function to run dynamically generated PHP code, especially if it can include user input. Look for safe alternatives like controlled function calls or explicit decision structures.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using eval() can lead to arbitrary code execution if malicious data is injected. It also makes code harder to debug and maintain.

items.title: Do Not Include Files Dynamically Without Validation
items.rule: Avoid passing untrusted values to include/require statements. If you need to include files based on external input, strictly validate that input (for example, using a whitelist of valid filenames).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Including files from user‐supplied paths without validation can lead to Local/Remote File Inclusion (LFI/RFI), allowing attackers to execute arbitrary code or access sensitive system files.

items.title: Use Secure Password Hashing Functions
items.rule: Do not use weak or generic hash algorithms (like MD5 or SHA1) for storing passwords. Use built‐in functions like password_hash() (with BCRYPT or Argon2) and password_verify(), which handle salting and secure algorithms automatically.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Old hash algorithms are vulnerable to brute‐force attacks and rainbow tables, making it easier to crack passwords. Modern password‐hashing functions include salts and iteration counts, making stored passwords much harder to compromise.

items.title: Do Not Expose Sensitive Credentials in Source Code
items.rule: Avoid hard‐coding credentials or sensitive information (like DB passwords, API tokens or secret keys) directly in your code. Use separate configuration files or environment variables to manage these values securely.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Embedded credentials can be accidentally exposed (e.g., in public repos), compromising system security. Storing them externally and referencing them securely prevents critical data leaks.

items.title: Protect Critical Forms with CSRF Tokens
items.rule: Implement anti‐CSRF tokens in forms performing sensitive actions (like state changes or deletions). Generate a unique token per session or request and validate it on the server before processing the form action.
items.whyIsThisImportant: CSRF tokens prevent malicious sites from forging form submissions on behalf of authenticated users. Without this protection, a user could unintentionally trigger unwanted actions by visiting a malicious page.

examples.snippet:
<?php
// No CSRF token used
if ($_POST['acao'] === 'delete') {
    deleteUser($_POST['id']);
}
?>

<?php
// Generate CSRF token
$_SESSION['csrf_token'] = bin2hex(random_bytes(32));
?>
<form method="POST">
  <input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="<?= $_SESSION['csrf_token'] ?>">
  <!-- other fields -->
</form>
<?php
// Validate CSRF token on submit
if (!isset($_POST['csrf_token']) || $_POST['csrf_token'] !== $_SESSION['csrf_token']) {
    die('Invalid CSRF token');
}
if ($_POST['acao'] === 'delete') {
    deleteUser($_POST['id']);
}
?>

items.title: Enable Secure Flags When Setting Cookies
items.rule: When sending session or other sensitive cookies, always enable the HttpOnly and Secure flags (and consider SameSite). HttpOnly prevents JavaScript access, and Secure ensures the cookie is only sent over HTTPS.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Without HttpOnly, malicious scripts (e.g., via XSS) can steal cookies. Without Secure, cookies may be transmitted in plaintext over HTTP, exposing them to interception. Proper cookie flags strengthen user session security.

items.title: Validate and Sanitize All Input Data
items.rule: Never trust data received from users, forms or requests. Always validate the expected format (e.g., numbers, emails) and sanitize/remove unexpected characters or content using appropriate techniques (filter_var, filter_input, etc.).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Unvalidated input may contain malicious commands or malformed content, leading to security issues like SQL injection and XSS. Validation and sanitization ensure your application only handles data in expected formats, reducing risks and errors.

examples.snippet:
<?php
$age = filter_input(INPUT_GET, 'idade', FILTER_VALIDATE_INT);
if (\$age === false) {
    // handle invalid input
} elseif (\$age > 18) {
    // proceed
}
?>

items.title: Use Cryptographically Secure Random Generators for Tokens
items.rule: For security tokens (session IDs, CSRF tokens, password‐reset links), use cryptographically secure random functions like random_bytes() or openssl_random_pseudo_bytes(), instead of predictable functions like rand() or mt_rand().
items.whyIsThisImportant: Common random functions are not suitable for security because they can produce predictable values. Predictable tokens allow attackers to guess session IDs or other sensitive tokens, compromising system security. Cryptographic generators ensure strong randomness.

items.title: Do Not Expose Error Details in Production
items.rule: Configure your application not to display detailed error messages (stack traces, PHP warnings) to end users in production. Instead, log errors for developers and show a generic message to users.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Detailed error messages can reveal sensitive information (directory structures, SQL queries, keys) useful to attackers. Logging errors internally and hiding details from users improves security and UX.

examples.snippet:
<?php
ini_set('display_errors', 1);
error_reporting(E_ALL);
// showing errors in production
?>

<?php
ini_set('display_errors', 0);
ini_set('log_errors', 1);
error_reporting(E_ALL);
// in production, errors are logged but not displayed
?>

items.title: Avoid Suppressing Errors with @
items.rule: Do not use the error‐suppression operator '@' before function calls or expressions. Instead, handle potential errors properly using return checks or exceptions (try/catch).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using '@' hides errors that may indicate serious issues, making bugs hard to find and fix. It also incurs a performance cost. Explicit error handling ensures code reliability.

examples.snippet:
<?php
$file = file_get_contents('data.txt');
if (\$file === false) {
    error_log('Failed to read data.txt');
}
?>

items.title: Enable strict_types Mode for Type Checking
items.rule: At the top of your PHP files (before any code), declare `declare(strict_types=1)`. This forces PHP to enforce the declared parameter and return types strictly, avoiding automatic type coercion.
items.whyIsThisImportant: With strict_types enabled, function calls with incorrect types throw TypeError instead of silently converting values. This helps catch type issues early in development, making code more robust and preventing unexpected behavior.

examples.snippet:
<?php
// file without strict_types
function sum(int \$a, int \$b): int {
    return \$a + \$b;
}
echo sum("5", "7");
?>

<?php
declare(strict_types=1);
function sum(int \$a, int \$b): int {
    return \$a + \$b;
}
echo sum("5", "7");
?>

items.title: Declare Visibility on Properties and Methods
items.rule: Always specify access levels (public, private, protected) when declaring class properties and methods. Don’t use the old `var` keyword for properties. Explicit visibility clarifies intent and prevents unintended access.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Specifying visibility improves code readability and maintenance by clearly defining public interfaces versus internal details. It enforces encapsulation and avoids accidental misuse of internal members.

examples.snippet:
<?php
class User {
    public string \$name;
    public function setName(string \$name): void {
        \$this->name = \$name;
    }
}
?>

items.title: Do Not Use goto for Control Flow
items.rule: Avoid using the `goto` statement for flow control. Instead, refactor code using loops (for, while) and conditional structures (if/else, switch) for clear and maintainable logic.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using `goto` can create spaghetti code that’s hard to follow and prone to errors. Conventional control structures make code more readable and easier to debug.

examples.snippet:
<?php
// Bad goto usage
if (!validate(\$data)) {
    goto error;
}
// ... other operations ...
error:
echo "An error occurred";
?>

<?php
if (!validate(\$data)) {
    echo "An error occurred";
} else {
    // ... other operations ...
}
?>

items.title: Avoid Relying on Global Variables
items.rule: Do not use global variables to share data across your program. Prefer passing values as function parameters or using configuration objects. Limiting variable scope makes code more predictable and less prone to side effects.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Global variables make it hard to trace where and how data changes, leading to conflicts and unpredictable behavior. Reducing globals improves encapsulation and testability.

examples.snippet:
<?php
// Using a global
$config = [];
function init() {
    global \$config;
    \$config['debug'] = true;
}
init();
if (\$config['debug']) {
    echo 'Debug on';
}
?>

<?php
// Pass config or use config object
function initConfig(array \$config): array {
    \$config['debug'] = true;
    return \$config;
}
\$config = initConfig(['debug' => false]);
if (\$config['debug']) {
    echo 'Debug on';
}
?>

items.title: Do Not Ignore Exceptions Without Handling
items.rule: Avoid empty catch blocks. When you catch an exception, implement meaningful handling: log the error, perform a fallback action, or rethrow after processing. Do not catch exceptions generically just to suppress them.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Silencing exceptions hides failures that may leave the system in an inconsistent state or make debugging difficult. Proper exception handling (even just logging) ensures you’re aware of issues and handle them appropriately.

examples.snippet:
<?php
try {
    \$data = file_get_contents('config.json');
} catch (Exception \$e) {
    error_log('Failed to load config: ' . \$e->getMessage());
    throw \$e; // or throw a custom exception
}
?>

items.title: Release External Resources After Use
items.rule: After using external resources (DB connections, open files, sockets), release them properly. Use fclose(), close database connections or finally blocks to ensure cleanup even if errors occur.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Each allocated resource consumes memory or system handles. Not releasing them can cause leaks or exhaustion (e.g., max DB connections). Proper cleanup ensures efficient operation and prevents resource‐related failures.

examples.snippet:
<?php
$handle = fopen('file.txt', 'r');
// ... read file
// never close handle
?>

<?php
\$handle = fopen('file.txt', 'r');
try {
    // ... read file
} finally {
    if (\$handle) {
        fclose(\$handle);
    }
}
?>

items.title: Do Not Use Deprecated or Removed Functions
items.rule: Avoid functions or extensions deprecated in recent PHP versions (e.g., mysql_* or preg_replace without proper delimiters). Use modern supported alternatives like PDO/MySQLi for DB or PCRE functions (preg_match, preg_replace with correct syntax).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Deprecated functions may be removed in future PHP releases and often lack security updates. Using up‐to‐date functions ensures compatibility, features, and security fixes, improving code longevity.

examples.snippet:
<?php
// use PDO instead of mysql_
$pdo = new PDO('mysql:host=localhost;dbname=test','user','pass');
?>

items.title: Keep Functions/Methods Single-Responsibility
items.rule: Write functions and methods so each does one well-defined task. If a function handles distinct steps (validation, processing, saving, notifying), refactor into smaller focused functions.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Single-responsibility functions are easier to read, test, and reuse. Smaller functions tend to have fewer bugs and simplify isolating issues, while making future enhancements cleaner.

examples.snippet:
<?php
function processData(\$data) {
    // validate
    if (!validate(\$data)) return false;
    // process
    \$result = calculate(\$data);
    // save
    saveToDb(\$result);
    // notify
    sendNotification(\$result);
    return true;
}
?>

<?php
function validateData(\$data): bool {
    // validation logic
}
function calculateResult(\$data) {
    // processing logic
}
function saveResult(\$res) {
    // persistence logic
}
function notifyUser(\$res) {
    // notification logic
}

if (validateData(\$input)) {
    \$res = calculateResult(\$input);
    saveResult(\$res);
    notifyUser(\$res);
}
?>

items.title: Avoid N+1 SQL Queries Inside Loops
items.rule: Do not perform multiple DB queries inside a loop for data you could fetch in one query. Instead, consolidate using IN clauses or JOINs to retrieve all needed data at once.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Looping queries (N+1 problem) causes excessive DB round-trips, degrading performance. Consolidating queries reduces load and improves response times, especially at scale.

examples.snippet:
<?php
\$ids = [1,2,3];
\$data = [];
foreach (\$ids as \$id) {
    \$res = \$pdo->query("SELECT * FROM items WHERE user_id = \$id");
    \$data[] = \$res->fetch();
}
?>

<?php
\$ids = [1,2,3];
\$list = implode(',', \$ids);
\$stmt = \$pdo->query("SELECT * FROM items WHERE user_id IN (\$list)");
\$data = \$stmt->fetchAll();
?>

items.title: Prefer Strict Comparisons (===) Over Loose (==)
items.rule: Use strict comparison operators (=== and !==) instead of loose (== and !=) in most cases. Strict comparisons check both type and value, avoiding PHP’s implicit type coercion pitfalls.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Loose comparisons can yield unexpected results due to type juggling (e.g., "0e123" == 0 is true). Using === ensures both type and value match, preventing subtle bugs and security issues.

items.title: Avoid Pointers to Interface Types
items.rule: Never use a pointer to an interface type. Pass interfaces as values, since an interface already provides reference semantics to the underlying data.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Interfaces are reference types; taking a pointer to an interface is unnecessary and can lead to confusion. It complicates function signatures without any benefit, and it doesn't change the fact that the underlying data can be a pointer.

items.title: Avoid Pointers to Slices (and Maps)
items.rule: Do not use pointers to slice or map types. Slices and maps are already reference types that point to underlying data, so they can be passed by value and still allow modifications to the contents.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using a pointer to a slice or map is redundant and can make snippet harder to understand. Since slices and maps already have reference semantics, passing them directly is simpler and less error-prone.

items.title: Copy Slices and Maps at Boundaries
items.rule: When accepting a slice or map from external snippet or returning one, consider copying it. This prevents unintentional modifications to the original data. For example, don't store a slice argument directly if the caller might modify it later; instead, make a copy for internal use.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Slices and maps hold references to underlying data. If you keep a reference to a slice or map provided by someone else, changes in one will affect the other, potentially causing bugs or data races. Copying ensures your snippet has its own independent data, improving safety and clarity.

items.title: Use defer to Release Resources
items.rule: Always use `defer` to release resources like files or locks when a function has multiple exit points. Open or lock resources and immediately defer their close/unlock to ensure they get released on every code path.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Deferring cleanup right after acquiring a resource ensures that no matter how the function exits (return or panic), the resource will be released. This prevents resource leaks and deadlocks caused by forgotten closes or unlocks.

items.title: Keep Channel Buffer Size Small (0 or 1)
items.rule: By default, use unbuffered channels or a buffer size of one. Only use larger buffers when necessary and carefully considered. Unbuffered channels (size 0) or a size of 1 are the most common; a large buffer can hide synchronization issues or lead to memory bloat if misused.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Channels coordinate goroutines. Unbuffered channels ensure strict handoff, and a buffer of one provides minimal queuing. Large buffers, if chosen poorly, might mask deadlocks or produce high memory usage as goroutines pile up sends, making bugs harder to detect.

items.title: Start Enums at 1, Reserve 0 for Default
items.rule: When using iota to create enums (constant sets), start from 1 and use 0 as a 'zero value' meaning undefined or default. This way, an uninitialized variable of that enum type will not accidentally hold a valid value.
items.whyIsThisImportant: In Go, an int's zero value is 0. If your enum uses 0 as a valid value, any variable of that enum type will default to that value even if never set, which can cause logic errors. Reserving 0 as an invalid or default state helps catch uninitialized usage.

examples.snippet:
type Role int
const (
    RoleUser Role = iota // 0
    RoleAdmin            // 1
)

var r Role
fmt.Println(r) // r is 0 -> interprets as RoleUser, even though we never set it

type Role int
const (
    _ Role = iota // 0 is skipped/reserved
    RoleUser      // 1
    RoleAdmin     // 2
)

var r Role
fmt.Println(r) // r is 0, which we treat as an undefined default

items.title: Use Appropriate Time Types (time.Time, time.Duration)
items.rule: Use the time package's types for handling time. For example, represent instants as `time.Time` and durations or time intervals as `time.Duration` instead of raw numeric types (int, int64).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using dedicated time types makes the code self-documenting and less error-prone. It prevents confusion over units (seconds vs milliseconds) and provides helpful methods. This leads to clearer and more correct time calculations.

items.title: Handle Errors Properly (Don't Ignore or Double-Handle)
items.rule: Never silently ignore an error, and avoid handling an error (e.g., logging it) and then still returning it. Either handle the error fully or propagate it upward, but not both.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Ignoring errors can lead to hidden bugs that are hard to diagnose. Logging an error and also returning it can result in duplicate logs or confusion about who is responsible for handling it. Each error should be dealt with in one place to maintain clarity and avoid surprises.

items.title: Wrap Errors with Context Information
items.rule: When returning an error from a function, include contextual information. Use `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` (or similar error wrapping) to add a message while preserving the original error.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Providing context in errors (such as what operation failed) makes debugging easier. Wrapping errors with `%w` retains the original error for inspection (using errors.Is/As), so the root cause isn't lost. Without context, you might get a generic error with no clue of its origin.

items.title: Use Consistent Error Message Style
items.rule: Error strings should start with a lowercase letter and not end with punctuation. They should state what went wrong in a concise, factual manner (for example, "file not found", not "File not found!").
items.whyIsThisImportant: Following Go conventions for error messages ensures that errors are consistent and can be combined in sentences. A lowercase, unpunctuated error string fits naturally when wrapped or printed, and maintains readability across the codebase.

items.title: Don't Panic on Regular Errors
items.rule: Reserve `panic` for unrecoverable situations (like invariant violations or truly fatal errors). For expected error conditions (file not found, invalid input, etc.), return an `error` to the caller instead of panicking.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Using panic for normal errors will crash the program and skip normal error handling logic. It makes the code less robust and harder to reuse. By returning errors, you allow the caller to decide how to handle the failure, leading to more graceful degradation and better reliability.

items.title: Handle Type Assertion Failures
items.rule: Use the 'comma, ok' idiom for type assertions to safely handle cases when the assertion fails. Only use a single-value type assertion (which panics on failure) if you are absolutely sure (by prior checks or program logic) that the interface holds the correct type.
items.whyIsThisImportant: If a type assertion is wrong, it causes a run-time panic. By using the two-value form (`value, ok := iface.(Type)`), your code can gracefully handle a bad type instead of crashing. This is especially important when dealing with interfaces like `interface{}` or `json.Unmarshalled` data where the type isn't guaranteed.

items.title: Avoid Mutable Global State
items.rule: Do not use global (package-level) variables for mutable state whenever possible. Instead, encapsulate state in structs or pass it as parameters. If you must use a global variable (for configuration or caching), protect it with mutexes if concurrent access is possible and document its usage.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Global mutable state can be modified from anywhere, which makes the program harder to understand, test, and maintain. It can introduce hidden dependencies between components and is prone to concurrency issues. Encapsulating state helps maintain invariants and makes code more modular.

items.title: Avoid Complex init() Functions
items.rule: Do not put complex logic or long-running tasks in package `init()` functions. Keep `init()` minimal (if you use it at all), such as simple registration of types or default variable initialization. Perform real setup in explicit functions (like in `main` or a setup function called by main).
items.whyIsThisImportant: `init()` runs before `main` and in an undefined order across packages, which can lead to unpredictable behavior and make testing harder. Complex init logic can slow down program start and hide dependencies. Keeping initialization explicit improves clarity and control over program startup.

items.title: Don't Leak Goroutines (No Unbounded Fire-and-Forget)
items.rule: Every goroutine you start should be managed. Avoid launching "fire-and-forget" goroutines that run indefinitely or without any synchronization. Provide a way to stop goroutines (using a cancel context or done channel) and/or track them (e.g., with sync.WaitGroup) to prevent leaks.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Uncontrolled goroutines can continue running and leak memory or other resources if the rest of the program moves on (for example, on shutdown or test completion). By managing goroutine lifecycles, you ensure they exit when expected, preventing resource leaks and unpredictable behavior.

items.title: Use sync.WaitGroup Correctly
items.rule: When using a WaitGroup to synchronize goroutines, call Add() before launching each goroutine and ensure each goroutine calls Done() exactly once. Finally, call Wait() to block until all goroutines have called Done(). Misordering Add/Done or mismatched calls will cause issues.
items.whyIsThisImportant: If you forget to Add before a goroutine or call Done more times than Add, the WaitGroup counter will go negative and panic. If you don't call Done or don't Wait properly, your program may hang. Proper usage of WaitGroup ensures no goroutines are missed and no extra signals occur, preventing panics and deadlocks.

examples.snippet:
var wg sync.WaitGroup

wg.Add(1)
go func() {
    defer wg.Done()
    doWork()
}()
wg.Wait()

items.title: Use Context for Cancellation and Timeouts
items.rule: Functions that perform external calls or long operations should accept a `context.Context` parameter and respect its cancellation. Use context timeouts or cancellation signals to stop work that is no longer needed.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Contexts allow you to avoid goroutine leaks and respond to cancellations (like user aborts or timeouts). Ignoring context means your function may continue doing work even if the result is no longer needed (e.g., user navigated away), wasting resources. It's essential for clean shutdowns and respecting deadlines.

items.title: Avoid Holding Large Backing Arrays via Small Slices
items.rule: Be c sem areful when slicing out a small portion of a large slice or array. The resulting slice still references the entire original array. If you only need the small portion long-term, copy that data into a new slice to avoid keeping the large array in memory.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Slicing a large array without copying can cause memory leaks or high memory usage since the garbage collector cannot reclaim the big array as long as a small slice references it. Copying the needed data prevents unexpectedly holding onto large memory blocks.

examples.snippet:
bigData := make([]byte, 1000000)
smallCopy := make([]byte, 10)
copy(smallCopy, bigData[:10])
// 'smallCopy' now contains the data and doesn't depend on the large array

items.title: Avoid Using defer in Hot Loops
items.rule: Don't place a defer inside a loop that iterates many times (especially if the loop is performance-critical). Defer calls won't run until the function returns and each call has a small overhead; instead, perform the operation directly in the loop when possible.
items.whyIsThisImportant: In a tight loop, deferring an operation on each iteration can lead to increased memory usage (each defer is stacked until function exit) and CPU overhead. This can slow down performance and delay resource release. It's more efficient to handle repetitive operations inline for large loops.

items.title: Don't Compare Addresses to nil
items.rule: Never compare the address of a variable to nil (e.g., `&x == nil`). To check if a pointer is nil, compare the pointer itself to nil. The address of a variable (`&x`) will always be non-nil if `x` exists.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Comparing `&x` to nil is a logical mistake; the condition will never be true (unless x is a nil pointer itself and you're taking the address of a pointer variable incorrectly). This bug can lead to code that never runs or always runs unexpectedly, since the condition is based on a false premise.

items.title: Docstrings required for public APIs (PEP 257 style)
items.rule: All public modules, classes, and functions must include a docstring immediately after the definition, using PEP 257 conventions (one-line summary + optional sections like Args, Returns, Raises).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Baseline documentation improves IDE help, searchability, and onboarding.

items.title: Keep docstrings consistent with signature and behavior
items.rule: Docstrings must match parameter names, optional defaults, return type, and raised exceptions; update the docstring in the same PR as any signature change.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents drift and misuse by readers and static tools.

examples.snippet:
def foo(x: int, y: int = 0) -> int:
 """Add x and y (default 0).
"""
 return x + y

def foo(x: int, y: int = 0) -> int:
 """Add a and b.
"""
 return x + y

items.title: JSDoc required for exported APIs
items.rule: All exported JS/TS functions, classes, and public members must include JSDoc with a summary and @param/@returns; include @throws when applicable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Improves IntelliSense, cross-language usage, and code navigation.

examples.snippet:
/* Multiply two numbers. @param {number} x @param {number} y @returns {number} /
export const mul = (x, y) => x * y;
export const mul = (x, y) => x * y;

items.title: Document async/Promise behavior and errors
items.rule: For async functions or functions returning Promise, document resolve value, rejection conditions, and usage with await; include @throws or @returns {Promise<Type>}.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Clarifies error handling and correct consumption patterns.

items.title: Javadoc required for public types and methods
items.rule: All public classes, interfaces, and methods must include Javadoc with a summary sentence, @param for each parameter, @return when not void, and @throws where applicable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Javadoc powers IDE help and stable API contracts.

examples.snippet:
/ Compute sum. @param a @param b @return sum /
public int add(int a, int b){ return a + b; }
public int add(int a, int b){ return a + b; }

items.title: Use @since/@deprecated with guidance
items.rule: When introducing or deprecating public APIs, include @since for introductions and @deprecated with migration notes and links for removals.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Communicates lifecycle and migration paths to consumers.

items.title: XML documentation for public APIs
items.rule: All public types and members must have XML documentation comments with <summary>, <param>, <returns> (if non-void), and <exception> when thrown.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Enables IntelliSense and consistent API usage across assemblies.

examples.snippet:
/
 * Find by id.
 * @param id External ID.
 /
public User Find(string id){ ... }

// Find by id
public User Find(string id){ ... }

items.title: Use <inheritdoc/> judiciously and document exceptions explicitly
items.rule: Use <inheritdoc/> to inherit docs for overrides/implementations, but explicitly add <exception> and behavior differences when applicable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Keeps docs DRY while making runtime differences clear.

items.title: Doc comments for packages and exported identifiers
items.rule: Every package must have a package comment, and each exported identifier must have a comment starting with the identifier name and a full-sentence summary.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Required for accurate godoc output and discoverability.

examples.snippet:
// Parse parses an ISO8601 timestamp.
func Parse(s string) (time.Time, error) { ... }

// parses time
func Parse(s string) (time.Time, error) { ... }

items.title: First sentence is a clear summary; document errors
items.rule: The first sentence should be a standalone summary used by godoc. For functions returning error, state error conditions succinctly.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Concise summaries and error docs improve skimmability and correctness.

examples.snippet:
// Open opens the file, returning ErrPermission when not allowed.
func Open(p string) (*os.File, error) { ... }

// Open file
func Open(p string) (*os.File, error) { ... }

items.title: Use item docs (///) and module/crate docs (!//) with examples
items.rule: Document public items with /// and modules/crates with //!; include minimal runnable examples that pass doctests where applicable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Rustdoc renders examples and validates them as doctests.

items.title: Document safety, panics, and errors
items.rule: When a function is unsafe, may panic, or returns Result, include sections # Safety, # Panics, and/or error conditions in the docs.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Explicit contracts prevent undefined behavior and misuse.

examples.snippet:
/// # Panics
/// Panics if index out of bounds.

items.title: Require an ADR for any architectural or cross-team decision
items.rule: For changes that affect architecture, data models, external APIs, security posture, deployment topology, or cost (>10%), create an ADR in docs/adr/ using the standard template (Context, Decision, Consequences) and link the PR and issue IDs.
items.whyIsThisImportant: ADR files in docs/adr/ capture the rationale behind impactful decisions so future contributors can understand trade-offs.

examples.snippet: README: 'we changed to gRPC' (no ADR, no issue/PR links)

items.title: Use canonical ADR filenames and numbering
items.rule: Store decision records under docs/adr/ with zero-padded incremental numbering and a kebab-case title: docs/adr/NNNN-title.md (e.g., docs/adr/0001-choose-postgresql.md).
items.whyIsThisImportant: A predictable docs/adr/ layout enables discoverability and tooling to index ADRs reliably.

items.title: Template must include Context, Decision, Consequences, and Status
items.rule: Every ADR must contain the sections: Context, Decision, Consequences, Status. Allowed Status values: proposed, accepted, deprecated, superseded.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Consistent sections make ADRs scannable and allow automation to validate lifecycle.

examples.snippet:
## Notes
We think Redis is nice

items.title: Link ADRs from PR descriptions and merge commits
items.rule: When a PR implements or changes a decision, include 'ADR: docs/adr/NNNN-title.md' in the PR description and reference it in the merge commit body.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Cross-linking PRs and docs/adr/ records keeps code and rationale synchronized.

items.title: Supersede instead of editing history
items.rule: Do not rewrite accepted ADRs. When reversing or replacing a decision, create a new ADR with Status: accepted and add 'Supersedes: NNNN' while marking the old ADR as 'Status: superseded by NNNN'.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Superseding preserves decision history and enables accurate audits.

examples.snippet: Edited prior ADR content to new decision

items.title: Reference external standards and benchmarks
items.rule: Where relevant, include links to RFCs, regulatory guidance, or benchmarks in the ADR (e.g., RFC 9110 for HTTP, PCI DSS sections), under a 'References' section.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Grounding decisions in standards improves rigor and onboarding.

examples.snippet: No references provided for protocol choice

items.title: Document measurable acceptance criteria
items.rule: Include clear, testable success metrics or SLOs in ADRs (e.g., p95 latency < 120ms, error rate < 0.1%) under a 'Measures of Success' section.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Explicit metrics connect architecture to outcomes and enable verification.

items.title: Capture security and privacy implications
