items.rule: Add a 'Security & Privacy' section in ADRs addressing threat model impacts, secrets handling, data retention, and PII processing; link to relevant runbooks and DPA if applicable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Documenting security/privacy consequences in ADRs reduces risk and speeds audits.

examples.snippet:
## Security & Privacy
PII minimized; keys via KMS

items.title: Declare rollout and migration plan
items.rule: Include a 'Rollout Plan' section with phases, feature flags, backout plan, and data migration steps; reference migration scripts and flag names.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Clear rollout details reduce production risk and align ops with design.

items.title: Record alternatives considered and trade-offs
items.rule: Add an 'Alternatives' section listing at least two plausible options with pros/cons and reasons for rejection.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Capturing alternatives prevents repeated debates and clarifies why the chosen path won.

items.title: Tie ADRs to versioning and deprecation notices
items.rule: For changes affecting public APIs or modules, include 'Versioning & Deprecation' with semantic version impact and deprecation timeline; link to CHANGELOG and deprecation notices.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Consumers need clear contracts; ADRs are the source of truth for version decisions.

items.title: Automate ADR linting in CI
items.rule: Add a CI job that validates docs/adr/ files: filename pattern, required sections, valid Status, presence of PR/issue links, and optional schema (YAML front matter).
items.whyIsThisImportant: CI checks keep ADR quality high and prevent drift.

items.title: Maintain an ADR index
items.rule: Keep docs/adr/README.md updated with a table of ADRs (number, title, status, date). Update the index in the same PR that adds/changes an ADR.
items.whyIsThisImportant: An index provides a single entry point for newcomers and supports audits.

items.title: Emit tamper-evident audit logs with required fields
items.rule: Every security-relevant action must append to an immutable, append-only audit log with fields: timestamp (UTC ISO8601), actor.user_id, actor.role, action, resource.id, result, trace_id, ip, user_agent. Logs must be signed or stored in WORM/immutable storage and forwarded to a SIEM.
items.whyIsThisImportant: SOC 2 requires auditability and tamper evidence; consistent fields enable investigations and access reviews.

items.title: Enforce TLS 1.2+ and HSTS on all external endpoints
items.rule: Public services must require TLSv1.2 or higher and set HSTS (max-age ≥ 15552000, includeSubDomains). Reject plaintext HTTP and weak ciphers; cookies must be Secure and HttpOnly with SameSite set.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Encryption in transit is a SOC 2 baseline control to protect confidentiality and integrity.

items.title: Encrypt sensitive data at rest with KMS and rotate keys
items.rule: Use envelope encryption (AES-256-GCM) with cloud KMS-managed CMKs for databases, object storage, and backups. Rotate CMKs at least annually and rotate DEKs per object/file.
items.whyIsThisImportant: SOC 2 expects strong encryption at rest and periodic key rotation to reduce blast radius.

items.title: Implement RBAC with least privilege and deny-by-default
items.rule: Authorize every request by role and resource scope. Policies must default-deny and require explicit allow; privileged roles (admin, auditor) must be rare and reviewed quarterly.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Least privilege and access reviews are core SOC 2 controls to limit unauthorized access.

items.title: Use a secrets manager and 90-day rotation policy
items.rule: All credentials (API keys, DB passwords, tokens) must be stored in a secrets manager with automatic rotation ≤ 90 days; forbid hardcoded or .env-committed secrets via CI checks.
items.whyIsThisImportant: SOC 2 requires secure secret handling and timely rotation to reduce exposure.

items.title: Classify data and enforce retention & deletion SLAs
items.rule: Tag records by data_class (PII, telemetry, auth) and enforce retention (e.g., PII ≤ 365 days unless legal hold). Provide scheduled deletion jobs and audit log entries.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Data minimization and lifecycle controls support SOC 2 privacy and confidentiality criteria.

examples.snippet: UPDATE users SET deleted_at=NOW() WHERE id=$1

items.title: Gate production changes with tickets and approvals
items.rule: All production deployments must reference a change ticket ID, peer approval, and linked rollout plan; write the change_id to the audit log on deploy.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Change management with approvals is required for SOC 2 to ensure controlled releases.

items.title: Generate and verify SBOM; fail build on critical CVEs
items.rule: Produce an SBOM (e.g., SPDX/CycloneDX) during CI and block releases if SCA finds CVEs ≥ High severity or disallowed licenses.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Supply chain visibility and vulnerability remediation are SOC 2 expectations.

items.title: Backups encrypted, tested, and monitored for RPO/RTO
items.rule: Create daily encrypted backups (KMS keys) with retention and geo-redundancy; run quarterly restore tests and record results in the audit log.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Availability and recoverability are part of SOC 2 trust criteria.

items.title: Mask PII and secrets in logs
items.rule: Before emitting logs, detect and redact PII and secret patterns (emails, tokens, keys). Replace values with consistent hashes or tokens; never log full credentials.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Logs often leave systems; SOC 2 requires confidentiality of sensitive data.

items.title: Harden session management with idle and absolute timeouts
items.rule: Set session idle timeout ≤ 15 minutes and absolute timeout ≤ 12 hours; cookies must be Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Lax or Strict; revoke sessions on password change.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Strong session controls reduce unauthorized access and meet SOC 2 security criteria.

items.title: Require step-up MFA for privileged operations
items.rule: Actions like role changes, key rotations, and exports of PII require MFA re-authentication within the last 5 minutes; record mfa_verified_at in the audit log.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Step-up auth reduces risk of session hijack and meets SOC 2 access control expectations.

items.title: Standardize incident response workflow and postmortems
items.rule: On Sev1/Sev2 incidents, capture timeline, root cause, remediation, and owner in a postmortem within 5 business days; link incident_id in all related commits and changes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Consistent IR improves resilience and evidences SOC 2 operational controls.

items.title: Enforce JIT access for production with break-glass logging
items.rule: Grant production access just-in-time via approved requests with scoped durations; break-glass access must trigger alerts and expanded audit logging.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Reduces standing privileges and provides evidence for access reviews in SOC 2.

items.title: Version and deprecate APIs with logged contract changes
items.rule: Expose explicit API versions (e.g., v1, v2). Breaking changes require a deprecation window and a CHANGELOG entry; log api_version in requests for traceability.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Controlled change and traceability support SOC 2 availability and integrity.

items.title: Define data export controls and watermarking
items.rule: All bulk exports of PII require approval, step-up MFA, rate limits, and watermarking of files with requestor and timestamp; record export_id in the audit log.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Controls on data exfiltration are expected for SOC 2 confidentiality.

items.title: Define change freezes and emergency override protocol
items.rule: Establish maintenance windows and freeze periods; emergency overrides require incident_id, approver, and post-facto review logged in the audit trail.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Controlled changes maintain availability and provide SOC 2 evidence.

items.title: Enforce HSTS and secure cookies in Express
items.rule: Use helmet to set HSTS and configure cookies with Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite. Reject HTTP by redirecting to HTTPS.
items.whyIsThisImportant: TLS and secure session cookies meet SOC 2 encryption-in-transit expectations.

items.title: Structured audit log middleware with trace IDs
items.rule: Attach a trace_id (e.g., from request header or generated UUID) and emit structured JSON logs for login, role change, and data export routes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Traceable actions enable SOC 2 audits and forensic investigations.

items.title: Require TLSv1.2+ and secure cookies
items.rule: Configure http.Server with tls.Config{MinVersion: tls.VersionTLS12} and set cookies with Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Strong TLS and session protections align with SOC 2 encryption controls.

items.title: Redact PII with log wrappers
items.rule: Wrap logger to filter emails and tokens using regex before logging; include trace_id in every entry.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents leakage of sensitive data and improves audit quality for SOC 2.

items.title: Enforce HTTPS, HSTS, and secure sessions
items.rule: Force HTTPS via middleware, set HSTS, and configure session cookies with secure=true, http_only=true, same_site=lax or strict in config/session.php.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Meets SOC 2 encryption-in-transit and session hardening requirements.

items.title: Filter sensitive parameters from logs
items.rule: Use $dontFlash in app/Http/Middleware/TrimStrings.php or logging processors to redact 'password', 'token', 'ssn', and emails.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Controls leakage of PII/secrets in logs for SOC 2 confidentiality.

items.title: force_ssl and parameter filtering
items.rule: Enable config.force_ssl = true; add filter_parameters to redact PII and secrets; use secure_same_site for cookies.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Meets SOC 2 transport security and log hygiene expectations.

items.title: Structured audit events
items.rule: Emit JSON audit events via ActiveSupport::Notifications with user_id, action, resource_id, and request_id.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Structured events improve SOC 2 auditability and incident response.

items.title: Enforce HTTPS, HSTS, and session policy
items.rule: Use Spring Security to require HTTPS, enable HSTS, set session fixation protection, and configure SameSite, Secure, HttpOnly cookies.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Aligns with SOC 2 encryption and session security.

items.title: Audit logging with MDC trace IDs
items.rule: Populate MDC with userId and traceId; include them in log pattern; log security events as JSON.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Consistent identifiers support SOC 2 investigations.

items.title: Require HTTPS and HSTS with Data Protection keys
items.rule: Use app.UseHsts(); app.UseHttpsRedirection(); store DataProtection keys in Azure Key Vault or KMS with rotation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: TLS and managed keys satisfy SOC 2 encryption requirements.

items.title: Structured audit events with ILogger
items.rule: Log security events via ILogger with properties {Action, UserId, ResourceId, Result, TraceId}; configure sink to JSON and send to SIEM.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Structured logs are evidence for SOC 2.

items.title: Security headers and request logging
items.rule: Add middleware to set HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, and secure cookies; log JSON with actor and trace_id for auth and admin routes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Hardening and auditing align with SOC 2 security criteria.

items.title: Enforce HTTPS and audit log calls
items.rule: Install HSTS in Ktor, require HTTPS redirects, and emit structured audit events for role changes with callId/traceId.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Transport security and auditable changes are SOC 2 essentials.

items.title: Add TLS and JSON audit middleware
items.rule: Serve via rustls with TLS1.2+ and add middleware to log JSON audit events with user_id and request_id; never log raw tokens.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Meets SOC 2 encryption and logging controls.

items.title: Propagate context with timeouts for external calls
items.rule: Use context.WithTimeout for HTTP and DB calls; log request deadlines and results; treat timeouts as audit-worthy events.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Controlled timeouts improve availability and detectability, aligning with SOC 2 availability criteria.

items.title: Block hardcoded secrets in CI with regex & entropy
items.rule: Add a CI step that fails builds if secret patterns (JWT, AWS keys) or high-entropy strings are detected in diffs.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents credential leakage and supports SOC 2 secure development practices.

items.title: Monolog processors to redact PII
items.rule: Configure Monolog to add a processor that redacts emails, tokens, and SSNs before writing logs; include request_id.
items.whyIsThisImportant: PII redaction in logs is essential for SOC 2 confidentiality.

items.title: Devise + MFA for privileged actions
items.rule: Require TOTP or WebAuthn MFA for admin areas; verify mfa_verified_at timestamp before sensitive routes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Step-up authentication supports SOC 2 access control.

items.title: Encrypt fields at application layer before persistence
items.rule: Use AES-GCM with keys from KMS to encrypt sensitive columns (e.g., SSN) before saving; store nonce and auth tag; never rely solely on disk encryption.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Defense-in-depth encryption supports SOC 2 confidentiality.

items.title: Cookie policy with SameSite and Secure defaults
items.rule: Configure CookiePolicyOptions to enforce SameSite=Lax or Strict, HttpOnly, and Secure for all auth cookies.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Proper cookie settings reduce session attacks and align with SOC 2.

items.title: Mask tokens before logging HTTP requests
items.rule: Intercept Authorization headers and redact bearer/JWT values; attach requestId to every log line.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Protects secrets in transit logs and supports traceability for SOC 2.

items.title: Encrypt secrets at rest with KMS client
items.rule: Use a KMS client to derive DEKs and encrypt sensitive blobs; rotate CMKs annually and re-encrypt on rotation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Managed key lifecycles meet SOC 2 encryption expectations.

items.title: Deny logging of secrets with a custom layer
items.rule: Implement a tracing layer that scans fields and redacts patterns (JWT, API keys, emails) before output.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents sensitive data exposure in logs for SOC 2.

items.title: Data retention job with classification
items.rule: Run a scheduled job that deletes telemetry after 30 days and anonymizes PII after the retention window; log deletions with data_class and count.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Enforces data lifecycle controls required by SOC 2.

items.title: Approvals for bulk export endpoints
items.rule: Protect /exports/ with role checks, fresh MFA, and rate limits; add requester and approved_by to the audit event.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Controls data exfiltration and provides evidence for SOC 2.

items.title: Disable debug and verbose errors in production
items.rule: Set APP_DEBUG=false and hide stack traces; send errors to a centralized logger with trace_id instead.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents leakage of sensitive details and aligns with SOC 2 secure operations.

items.title: mTLS for internal service-to-service traffic
items.rule: Configure Spring Boot to require client certificates for internal APIs (clientAuth=REQUIRE) and restrict CAs; log peer certificate subject in audit events.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Strong service authentication and encryption support SOC 2 security.

items.title: Fingerprint assets and cache immutably
items.rule: All static JS/CSS/font/image files MUST use content-hashed filenames (e.g., app.9c1a7b.js) and be served with "Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable". HTML and other non-fingerprinted documents MUST be served with "Cache-Control: no-cache" (or equivalent) to enable conditional revalidation.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Hashed filenames guarantee cache-busting on deploys and enable safe year-long immutable caching for fast repeat loads.

items.title: Compress text assets with Brotli/Gzip and set Vary
items.rule: Serve text-based assets (JS, CSS, JSON, SVG) with Brotli (br) when the client sends "Accept-Encoding: br" and fallback to gzip. Always set "Vary: Accept-Encoding" and do NOT compress already-compressed formats (e.g., .png, .jpg, .woff2).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Brotli significantly reduces transfer sizes for text assets and the Vary header prevents cache poisoning across clients.

items.title: Preload critical fonts and use font-display: swap
items.rule: Preload render-blocking WOFF2 fonts using <link rel="preload" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin> and define @font-face with "font-display: swap". Only preload fonts used above the fold.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Proper font preloading prevents layout shifts and enables faster first paint without FOIT.

items.title: Serve responsive images with modern formats and lazy-load
items.rule: Provide AVIF/WebP fallbacks via with width/height attributes, meaningful sizes, and "loading=lazy" plus "decoding=async". Avoid shipping oversized PNG/JPEG when AVIF/WebP is supported.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Responsive modern formats cut bytes and improve LCP while preserving quality.

items.title: Restrict production source maps
items.rule: Do not expose full, unminified source maps publicly in production. If source maps are required for error tracking, serve them behind authentication or IP allowlists and set "X-Robots-Tag: noindex".
items.whyIsThisImportant: Public source maps leak implementation details and ease reverse engineering.

items.title: Immutable cache for hashed assets, no-cache for HTML
items.rule: In Express, use serve-static with setHeaders to apply "Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable" for files matching /\.[0-9a-f]{8,}\./ and "Cache-Control: no-cache" for HTML. Also set correct Content-Type.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Differentiating asset types ensures fast repeat loads while keeping documents revalidatable.

items.title: ETag/Last-Modified for non-immutable assets
items.rule: For Go static servers, set immutable Cache-Control for hashed files; otherwise set "Cache-Control: no-cache" and support conditional GET via ETag or Last-Modified using file info.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Validators prevent unnecessary transfers while keeping clients fresh.

items.title: Versioned static resources with long cache
items.rule: Enable content versioning and long-lived caching via ResourceChain with VersionResourceResolver and set Cache-Control to public, max-age=31536000 for static locations; keep HTML templates non-immutable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Versioned URLs allow aggressive caching without risking stale assets.

items.title: StaticFileOptions with cache policy and types
items.rule: Configure UseStaticFiles with OnPrepareResponse to set "Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable" for hashed assets and "no-cache" for HTML; ensure correct Content-Type (e.g., "font/woff2", "text/css").
items.whyIsThisImportant: Explicit headers and MIME types avoid stale assets and incorrect rendering.

items.title: Correct Content-Type and cache headers for assets
items.rule: For PHP-served assets, emit the precise Content-Type (e.g., text/css; charset=utf-8, application/javascript, font/woff2) and set immutable caching for hashed files; never apply immutable caching to HTML.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Accurate MIME types and tailored caching improve compatibility and speed.

items.title: Cache and compression for static resources
items.rule: In Kotlin Ktor, install CachingHeaders and Compression. Set immutable caching for hashed resources and no-cache for HTML; include "Vary: Accept-Encoding".
items.whyIsThisImportant: Built-in Ktor features simplify correct caching and compression for static assets.

items.title: Immutable cache and Vary for precompressed files
items.rule: Serve precompressed .br/.gz variants when the client sends Accept-Encoding, set "Vary: Accept-Encoding", and apply immutable caching for hashed assets; keep HTML revalidatable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Precompressed delivery reduces CPU and latency; Vary ensures correct CDN caching.

items.title: Set correct MIME types for all static assets
items.rule: Serve assets with accurate Content-Type (e.g., text/css, application/javascript, image/avif, image/webp, font/woff2, image/svg+xml) and include charset for text types. Do not default to text/plain or application/octet-stream.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Correct MIME types ensure proper rendering, security, and cache behavior.

items.title: Freeze time and random sources for deterministic tests
items.rule: In unit and integration tests, call a time-freeze utility and set a fixed random seed before exercising the system under test; unfreeze after assertions.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Deterministic time and RNG remove flakiness and make failures reproducible.

examples.snippet: const ts = now(); const id = random(); // flaky

items.title: No live network or DB in unit tests—use fakes/mocks
items.rule: Unit tests must stub or fake external HTTP/DB calls; allow real I/O only in integration/e2e tests tagged accordingly.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Live I/O introduces slowness, nondeterminism, and external failures unrelated to the code.

items.title: Behavioral changes must add or update tests
items.rule: If files under src/ change behavior, the PR must include tests/ updates or a PR description line no-tests: <reason> explaining why tests are unaffected.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents silent regressions and enforces test coverage of changed behavior.

examples.snippet: PR_DESCRIPTION: no-tests: refactor-only (rename, no behavior change)

files_changed:
- src/service.ts
# tests/ unchanged and no rationale

items.title: Enforce minimum coverage in CI
items.rule: CI must fail the build when line coverage drops below the project threshold (e.g., 99%); publish coverage reports as artifacts.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Automated gates prevent coverage regressions and make quality visible.

items.title: Jest: use async/await and resolves/rejects instead of done()
items.rule: For Promises, return/await the expectation and prefer await expect(p).resolves|rejects...; do not use the callback done().
items.whyIsThisImportant: Avoids false positives and ensures the test actually waits for async completion.

items.title: Go: parallel-safe tests with t.Parallel and t.TempDir
items.rule: Mark tests that don't mutate shared state with t.Parallel() and use t.TempDir() for filesystem isolation; avoid package-level globals.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Parallelism speeds suites and isolation prevents hidden data races.

examples.snippet: var shared bytes.Buffer // used by tests

items.title: JUnit 5: assertThrows and timeouts for slow paths
items.rule: Use assertThrows for exception cases and annotate long-running tests with @Timeout to bound execution; use @TempDir for file I/O.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Explicit exception and time boundaries reduce flakiness and hangs.

items.title: xUnit: async tests return Task and avoid Thread.Sleep
items.rule: In async tests, return Task and await the operation; never use Thread.Sleep—use cancellation tokens or timeouts with Assert.ThrowsAsync where needed.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Proper async patterns prevent deadlocks and reduce flakiness.

examples.snippet: public async Task TestX(){ await op(); }

items.title: PHPUnit: use data providers and type-strict assertions
items.rule: Prefer data providers for parameterized cases and use assertSame/assertNotSame for type-strict checks; avoid loose equals.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Catches subtle type issues and reduces repetitive tests.

items.title: RSpec: stub external HTTP and assert on effects
items.rule: Use WebMock or equivalent to stub external requests in specs and prefer effect-based matchers (e.g., change(Model, :count)) over brittle value guesses.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Isolation and behavior-focused assertions reduce flakiness.

items.title: Kotlin coroutines: use runTest and avoid Thread.sleep
items.rule: Test suspend code with kotlinx-coroutines-test runTest {} and virtual time (advanceUntilIdle()); do not block with Thread.sleep.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Virtual time makes async tests fast and deterministic.

items.title: Rust async tests: use #[tokio::test] and avoid unwrap()
items.rule: For async code, annotate with #[tokio::test] (or async runtime in use) and return Result from tests; never use unwrap()/expect() in assertions.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Proper async runtime and error propagation yield clearer failures.

items.title: Terraform state must use a remote backend with locking
items.rule: Configure a remote backend with state locking (e.g., S3 + DynamoDB table or AzureRM blob with leases). Do not use local backend for shared environments.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Remote, locked state prevents concurrent writes and corruption during team applies.

items.title: Tag/label every resource with owner, env, and cost-center
items.rule: All Terraform/Kubernetes resources must include standard tags/labels: owner, env, cost-center (and application if available).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Consistent metadata enables cost allocation, searchability, and cleanup.

examples.snippet:
metadata:
 labels:
 owner: platform-team
 env: prod
 cost-center: cc-1234

items.title: Pods must run as non-root with read-only root filesystem
items.rule: Set securityContext.runAsNonRoot=true, runAsUser!=0, and readOnlyRootFilesystem=true on pods/containers.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Reduces blast radius and prevents privileged writes if compromised.

items.title: Set CPU/memory requests and limits for all containers
items.rule: Every container must specify resources.requests and resources.limits for cpu and memory.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents noisy-neighbor issues and enables reliable scheduling.

examples.snippet:
resources:
 requests:
 cpu: 200m
 memory: 256Mi
 limits:
 cpu: 500m
 memory: 512Mi

items.title: Default-deny NetworkPolicy required when adding a namespace
items.rule: If a PR introduces workloads in a namespace that lacks a default-deny NetworkPolicy, add one plus explicit allow rules.
items.whyIsThisImportant: A default-deny baseline limits lateral movement and unintended traffic.

examples.snippet:
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
 name: default-deny
 namespace: payments

kind: Deployment
metadata:
 name: payments-api
 namespace: payments

items.title: Add a PodDisruptionBudget for HA Deployments
items.rule: For Deployments with replicas ≥ 2, include a PodDisruptionBudget with minAvailable or maxUnavailable set.
items.whyIsThisImportant: PDBs preserve availability during voluntary disruptions such as node drains.

items.title: Validate Terraform variables with types and constraints
items.rule: All variables must declare a type, default (when sensible), and validation blocks; mark sensitive inputs with sensitive=true.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Strongly-typed, validated inputs prevent unsafe applies and secrets leakage.

items.title: Pin providers/modules and commit .terraform.lock.hcl
items.rule: Use version constraints for required_providers and modules, and include .terraform.lock.hcl in PRs that change dependencies.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Pinned versions and lockfiles ensure reproducible plans and safer rollbacks.

items.title: Pin container images by digest in Helm/K8s
items.rule: Use immutable image digests (repo@sha256:...) instead of mutable tags for production workloads.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Digests provide deterministic deployments and avoid surprise upgrades.

items.title: Pulumi TS: secrets must use Config.requireSecret
items.rule: In Pulumi TypeScript programs, load sensitive values with new Config().requireSecret and pass as Secret<T>, never plain strings.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Secrets stay encrypted in state and are redacted in logs.

items.title: Pulumi Go: protect critical resources from destroy
items.rule: For stateful/critical infra (state buckets, databases), create resources with pulumi.Protect(true) to block accidental destroy.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Protection guards against destructive updates and human error.

items.title: Require a plan artifact and policy checks in CI
items.rule: CI must run fmt/validate, produce and upload a plan artifact (e.g., terraform plan -out plan.bin), run policy-as-code (e.g., tfsec/checkov/conftest), and gate apply on manual approval for prod.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Automated, reproducible plans and policy checks reduce risk before apply.

items.title: Disallow public ingress without explicit allowlist
items.rule: For Ingress/Service of type LoadBalancer, require host/path allowlist and TLS; forbid 0.0.0.0/0 exposure without WAF or Auth.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents accidental public exposure of internal services.

items.title: Helm charts must template resource names and labels consistently
items.rule: Use template helpers to set metadata.name and labels (app.kubernetes.io/) consistently across templates; prohibit hardcoded mismatches.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Consistent naming/labels improve upgrades, selectors, and observability.

items.title: Keep a single lockfile at the repo root
items.rule: Permit exactly one lockfile at the monorepo root (pnpm-lock.yaml, yarn.lock, or package-lock.json). Forbid lockfiles inside workspace packages. Validate against PR changes and existing tree.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Root-only lockfiles avoid divergent dependency graphs and flaky builds in workspaces.

items.title: Enforce a single package manager via packageManager field
items.rule: Root package.json must define the packageManager field (e.g., "pnpm@9.x") and the repo must not contain other lockfiles or tool config (e.g., yarn.lock, package-lock.json) conflicting with that choice.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Guarantees consistent tooling and cache behavior across all workspaces.

items.title: Tighten workspace globs in pnpm-workspace.yaml
items.rule: In pnpm-workspace.yaml, explicitly list workspace globs (e.g., "apps/", "packages/") and exclude build outputs (e.g., "/dist", "/build"). Forbid catch-all patterns like "*" or "".
items.whyIsThisImportant: Precise workspace selection prevents accidental inclusion of tooling or build artifacts as packages.

items.title: Turbo pipeline must declare dependencies and outputs
items.rule: In turbo.json, each task must set dependsOn (e.g., "^build") and outputs (e.g., "dist/"). Require cache:true for build/test/lint pipelines unless explicitly justified.
items.whyIsThisImportant: A correct build graph and declared outputs enable incremental builds and consistent caching across workspaces.

items.title: Require Changeset for publishable package changes
items.rule: If a PR modifies a workspace with "private": false in its package.json, enforce a .changeset/*.md entry describing semver impact for each affected package.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Changesets keep versioning and release notes consistent across multiple packages.

items.title: Forbid cross-package relative imports in JS/TS
items.rule: In JS/TS workspaces, imports must use workspace package names (as defined in each package.json and exports) rather than relative paths crossing package boundaries.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Stable package imports preserve boundaries and avoid duplicate module copies and cyclic graphs.

items.title: Enable TS project references for depended packages
items.rule: When a TS package depends on another workspace package, tsconfig.json must set "composite": true and include a "references" entry to the dependency; root must aggregate paths via tsconfig.base.json if used.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Project references give correct incremental builds and type isolation in monorepos.

items.title: Use go.work at repo root instead of replace in modules
items.rule: Maintain a single go.work at the monorepo root listing all local modules via use directives. Forbid go.mod replace directives that point to sibling modules (e.g., ../pkg) in workspace modules.
items.whyIsThisImportant: go.work provides a consistent build graph and avoids ad-hoc replaces that break tooling.

items.title: Centralize Rust deps with workspace.dependencies
items.rule: In the root Cargo.toml, define [workspace] members and manage shared versions under [workspace.dependencies]. Member Cargo.toml files must not pin versions for crates declared centrally.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Centralized dependencies avoid version skew and duplicate builds across crates.

items.title: Gradle: define modules in settings.gradle and use version catalogs
items.rule: List all included builds/modules in settings.gradle and manage versions via gradle/libs.versions.toml (version catalogs). Forbid hardcoded versions and repo declarations in module build.gradle files.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Centralized module graph and versions make multi-module builds reproducible.

items.title: Central Package Management for .NET
items.rule: Use Directory.Packages.props at the repo root to declare PackageVersion entries. Workspace .csproj files must reference packages without embedding Version attributes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Central management prevents version drift across solutions in a monorepo.

items.title: Disallow per-package registries and proxies
items.rule: Registry configuration (e.g., .npmrc, .yarnrc.yml) must live at the monorepo root. Forbid .npmrc files inside workspace packages overriding registry auth or URL.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Single source of truth for registries reduces supply-chain and publishing mistakes.

items.title: SBT: keep scalaVersion and versions centralized
items.rule: Define scalaVersion and library versions in version.sbt or ThisBuild in build.sbt; modules must not override scalaVersion or pin conflicting dependency versions locally.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Centralized versions keep cross-module binary compatibility and deterministic builds.

items.title: Do not log PHI; mask and drop sensitive fields
items.rule: Never write Protected Health Information (PHI/ePHI) to logs. Redact fields like name, SSN, MRN, DOB, address, diagnoses, and lab results; store only non-identifying metadata and a stable request trace id. If logging is required for troubleshooting, replace values with consistent tokens and record access separately in the audit log.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA requires safeguarding PHI; logging PHI creates uncontrolled copies and disclosure risk.

items.title: Encrypt ePHI at rest with KMS-managed AES-256 and envelope keys
items.rule: Before persisting ePHI, encrypt using a data key protected by a Key Management Service (KMS). Use authenticated encryption (AES-256-GCM or equivalent), rotate keys, and store the key id and algorithm with the record.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA technical safeguards require protecting ePHI at rest with strong, auditable encryption and key management.

examples.snippet:
cipher, kid := kms.Encrypt(ctx, "alias/phi-data", ephi)
db.Save(Record{Blob: cipher, KMSKeyID: kid})

items.title: Enforce “minimum necessary” data selection in queries
items.rule: When reading from PHI tables, project only authorized fields and avoid SELECT *. Implement whitelists per role and document them. Reject requests lacking a Purpose-Of-Use justification.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA’s minimum necessary standard reduces exposure by limiting PHI to what’s required for a task.

examples.snippet: SELECT initials, dob FROM patients WHERE id=?

items.title: Write immutable audit logs for all ePHI access
items.rule: For any handler that reads or writes ePHI, write an append-only audit record with user id, patient id, action (READ_PHI/WRITE_PHI), purpose-of-use, timestamp, and request id. Prevent deletion or mutation of audit entries.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA requires traceability of PHI access; robust audit trails support detection and incident response.

items.title: Introduce an auditable “break-glass” path only with justification
items.rule: Emergency access to ePHI must go through a dedicated break_glass path that requires reason, approver, limited time window, and automatic post-incident review. The PR must include changes to the audit log schema and runbook.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA allows emergency access but mandates accountability and post-access review.

examples.snippet:
- Add /break_glass with reason+approver
- Update audit schema and runbook

items.title: Enforce TLS 1.2+ and HSTS for all ePHI endpoints
items.rule: Applications serving ePHI must require HTTPS, disable insecure ciphers, and enable HSTS with preload and includeSubDomains. Reject cleartext HTTP requests.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA transmission security requires protecting ePHI in transit.

items.title: De-identify analytics and block PHI egress to third parties
items.rule: Any PR that adds or modifies telemetry/analytics must prove that no PHI leaves the boundary. Use irreversible tokenization or hashing (HMAC with secret salt) for identifiers and document the data dictionary.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Sending PHI to vendors without BAAs risks violations; de-identification reduces exposure.

items.title: Protect backups/exports of ePHI with KMS and restricted buckets
items.rule: Write ePHI backups to storage with server-side encryption (SSE-KMS) and least-privilege access; disallow public ACLs and cross-account access without a BAA. Record the KMS key id in metadata.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Backups contain full datasets; encryption and access controls prevent bulk disclosure.

items.title: Check consent and role-based authorization before returning ePHI
items.rule: Handlers must verify the requester’s role and a valid consent/relationship (e.g., care team assignment) before disclosing ePHI; include Purpose-Of-Use in the decision and audit the outcome.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA requires access control and disclosure tracking to limit ePHI to authorized use.

items.title: Implement retention policy and purge jobs for ePHI
items.rule: Changes that create new ePHI stores must also include retention configuration and a scheduled purge job aligned to policy (e.g., 6 years). Document tables/collections covered and add tests for TTL behavior.
items.whyIsThisImportant: HIPAA requires policies for retention and disposal; automated purges reduce residual risk.

examples.snippet:
retention.yaml: { table: phi_events, ttl: 6y }
cron: purge_phi_events @daily

items.title: Load PHI-related secrets from a secret manager, never from code
items.rule: Access tokens, HMAC salts, and KMS keys for PHI flows must come from a secret manager or encrypted credentials store; forbid committing secrets or .env files to the repo.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Leaked secrets can expose PHI and encryption material.

items.title: Require MFA for admin actions touching ePHI
items.rule: Endpoints under /admin or privileged routes that can view or export ePHI must enforce multi-factor authentication and recent re-auth (e.g., within 15 minutes).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Stronger auth reduces risk of unauthorized disclosure of PHI.

items.title: Persist consent per purpose with immutable history
items.rule: Consent must be stored per user and per purpose with lawful_basis, version, granted_at, source, and optional revoked_at. Do not overwrite or store a single boolean flag. (GDPR Art. 7, purpose limitation)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Granular, auditable consent enables purpose limitation and proof of consent/revocation.

examples.snippet: UPDATE users SET consent=true WHERE id=$1;

items.title: Enforce retention with explicit TTL per data category
items.rule: PII tables must include an expires_at (or retention_policy) column and a scheduled deletion/archival job aligned to data category. No indefinite retention. (GDPR Art. 5(1)(e))
items.whyIsThisImportant: Limits exposure and fulfills storage limitation obligations.

examples.snippet: DELETE FROM events WHERE expires_at <= NOW();
SELECT COUNT() FROM events WHERE created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '5 years'; -- but never deleting

items.title: Tag PII in schemas and events for RoPA
items.rule: Mark columns and event fields with pii and purpose metadata; prefer hashed or tokenized variants in telemetry. Use these tags to auto-generate Records of Processing Activities (RoPA). (GDPR Art. 30)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Discoverability and auditability of personal data flows are mandatory.

items.title: PII-introducing PRs require DPIA & RoPA updates
items.rule: If a PR adds new PII fields (e.g., email, phone, address, dob, national_id), include links to updated DPIA and RoPA, migration with retention, and masking rules. Add a checklist item confirming lawful_basis. (GDPR Art. 35, Art. 30)
items.whyIsThisImportant: High-risk processing must be assessed and documented at change time.

examples.snippet: Adds phone TEXT with no docs or retention

items.title: Idempotent Right to Erasure across all stores
items.rule: Provide a single idempotency-keyed erasure workflow that scrubs PII in primary DB, caches, search indices, and object storage; emit an audit event with erasure_request_id. (GDPR Art. 17)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Erasure must be complete, consistent, and auditable.

examples.snippet: eraseUser(userId); // no idempotency, no cache/search cleanup

items.title: Redact PII in logs and metrics by default
items.rule: Logging/metrics must redact or hash personal data; attach lawful_basis and purpose to diagnostic context; forbid raw PII in logs. (GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) data minimization)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Logs are high-volume replicas; leakage risk is high.

items.title: Gate analytics cookies on explicit consent (Web)
items.rule: Only set analytics/marketing cookies if consent.purposes.analytics === true and consent.version matches current policy; re-check on version bump. (GDPR Art. 7, ePrivacy)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Tracking requires prior, informed consent in many jurisdictions.

items.title: Transactional erasure with tombstone event
items.rule: Wrap PII scrubbing and session invalidation in a single DB transaction; publish a gdpr.erased event with subject ID and timestamp after commit.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents partial erasure and ensures downstream consistency.

examples.snippet: db.Exec("DELETE FROM users WHERE id=$1", id)

items.title: Log redaction via Monolog processor
items.rule: Register a Monolog processor to mask emails/phones and attach pii_redacted:true; forbid direct logging of raw identifiers.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Centralized redaction prevents accidental leakage across codepaths.

items.title: Encrypt PII at rest using application-layer crypto
items.rule: Encrypt sensitive columns (e.g., email, phone) with managed keys; ensure DB connection enforces TLS. Store only ciphertext; compare via deterministic token/hashes when needed. (GDPR Art. 32)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Encryption reduces breach impact and supports data protection by design.

items.title: Portable DSAR export with signed URL and TTL
items.rule: Provide an authenticated endpoint that assembles a user's data into a structured JSON export and returns a short-lived signed URL (≤24h). Include integration tests in the PR. (GDPR Art. 20)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Ensures data portability while minimizing exposure window.

examples.snippet: return ResponseEntity.ok(csvBody); // inline data, no TTL

items.title: Background retention worker for PII cleanup
items.rule: Implement a scheduled worker to delete or anonymize records past expires_at, with metrics on deletions and failures. Dry-run mode allowed only in non-prod.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Automates storage limitation and provides proof of enforcement.

items.title: Cross-border transfers annotated with SCC references
items.rule: When configuring processors outside the EEA, annotate the config with transfer_mechanism (e.g., SCCs) and link to the signed SCC document; prevent enablement without this metadata. (GDPR Art. 46)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Ensures lawful international data transfers are documented and enforced in code.

items.title: Mask PAN and exclude SAD from logs
items.rule: Never emit Primary Account Number (PAN) or Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD: CVV/CVC, full track data, PIN) to application or audit logs. Per PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 3 and 10, always mask PAN as first6last4 and fully redact SAD before logging.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Logs are broadly accessible; leaking CHD/SAD violates PCI DSS and creates high breach risk.

items.title: Disallow CVV/CVC persistence anywhere
items.rule: Never store CVV/CVC or other SAD after authorization in databases, caches, files, or telemetry. Schema/models must not define fields for CVV/CVC; reject writes containing cvv/cvc keys. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 3.2.1)
items.whyIsThisImportant: SAD storage is prohibited; accidental retention is a common path to PCI violations.

items.title: Use client tokenization; backend never receives PAN
items.rule: For web/mobile payment forms, send PAN and CVV directly to the PCI-compliant gateway to obtain a single-use token; submit only the token to your backend. Validate that backend APIs reject requests containing PAN/SAD. (PCI DSS scoping & SAQ A)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Keeping PAN out of your environment reduces PCI scope and breach impact.

examples.snippet: const token = await gateway.tokenize({pan, cvv}); formData.append('token', token)

items.title: Enforce TLS 1.2+ and strict cert verification to payment gateway
items.rule: All outbound connections that may carry CHD/PAN tokens must enforce TLS >= 1.2, validate hostname/chain, and optionally pin gateway SPKI. Disable insecure ciphers/versions. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 4)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Strong transport encryption prevents interception of CHD and tokens.

items.title: Encrypt transient PAN with AES-GCM using KMS keys and zeroize
items.rule: If PAN must be handled transiently, encrypt in memory with AES-256-GCM using a KMS/HSM-provided key, store only ciphertext or tokens, and zeroize plaintext buffers immediately. Rotate keys per policy. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 3)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Strong cryptography and key management limits blast radius if memory or storage is exposed.

items.title: Display PAN as first6last4 only
items.rule: Render PAN only in truncated form (first 6 and last 4) and never expose full PAN, CVV, or expiration data together. Apply the masking helper at all presentation points including emails and PDFs. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 3.3)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Partial display reduces exposure while supporting customer service workflows.

items.title: Gate CHD token access with explicit PCI roles
items.rule: Any code path that reads, decrypts, or exchanges PAN tokens must require an explicit authorization policy (e.g., role "pci:read_token") and log access without PAN. Deny by default. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 7 & 10)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Least-privilege access reduces insider and lateral-movement risk for CHD.

items.title: Emit append-only audit logs for CHD access without PAN
items.rule: On every create/read/update/delete of CHD or tokens, write a structured audit event (who, what, when, result) without full PAN, including only pan_last4. Persist to an append-only/immutable sink. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 10)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Tamper-evident audit trails enable incident response and forensics.

items.title: Block real PAN/SAD in fixtures and test data
items.rule: Reject PRs adding real PAN/CVV in fixtures, seeds, or mocks. Only use Luhn-valid test PANs from the gateway or opaque tokens (e.g., tok_) and never include CVV. Add a check to fail if a PAN regex is matched. (PCI DSS data minimization)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Test data leaks propagate widely and are often copied to non-PCI systems.

items.title: Redact CHD in CI and fail builds on PAN patterns
items.rule: CI logs must redact secrets and CHD; disable shell xtrace, and add a job that fails if PAN regex (e.g.,  [0-9]{13,19}  with Luhn) appears in logs/artifacts. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 10 & CI hygiene)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Build systems aggregate logs and artifacts; leakage here is pervasive and long-lived.

items.title: Pin FIPS-grade crypto deps and produce SBOM
items.rule: Pin cryptography libraries to vetted versions (e.g., BouncyCastle FIPS) and generate a CycloneDX SBOM as part of the build. Reject PRs introducing floating or insecure versions. (PCI DSS 4.0 Req. 6 & supply chain)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Pinned, auditable dependencies reduce the chance of weak crypto or supply chain attacks.

items.title: Deny caching of payment pages and responses
items.rule: Set HTTP headers for payment entry points and CHD-adjacent responses to prevent storage: Cache-Control: no-store, Pragma: no-cache, and appropriate privacy headers. Ensure intermediaries cannot cache PAN-related flows.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Caching CHD-bearing pages increases exposure across clients and proxies.

items.title: Validate gateway responses and avoid storing PAN substitutes long-term
items.rule: Accept only short-lived tokens from the gateway (JWT/JWE or opaque) and validate signature/expiry; do not persist tokens beyond business need. Never attempt to reconstruct PAN from tokens. (PCI DSS data minimization)
items.whyIsThisImportant: Limiting token lifetime and storage reduces scope and lateral risk.

items.title: Validate implementation against ticket Acceptance Criteria
items.rule:
If the PR references a ticket (e.g., 'Refs: ABC-123' or a Linear/Jira/Asana/ClickUp/Trello link), verify the diff covers each Acceptance Criterion.
- If a ticketing MCP is available: fetch the ticket and list AC → code/tests mapping.
- If no MCP is available or the ticket is inaccessible: ask the author to paste the Acceptance Criteria in the PR description.
Flag any missing criteria, unhandled edge cases, or partial implementations before human review.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents shipping incomplete work and keeps requirements-to-code traceability.

examples.snippet: Refs: ABC-123 (no AC mapping, missing behaviors)
AC coverage checklist + links to code/tests/docs

items.title: Bugfix PRs must match the real production failure (root cause, not band-aid)
items.rule:
If the PR claims to fix a specific issue (e.g., 'Fixes #123' / 'Fix PAY-123'), validate it against the real production error.
- If an observability MCP is available (Sentry/Datadog/Bugsnag): fetch the event/stack trace and confirm the change addresses the root cause.
- Require a regression test (or a clearly documented reason why a test cannot be added).
Call out fixes that only hide symptoms (catch-and-ignore, broader retries, defaulting values) without removing the underlying failure mode.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents repeat incidents by ensuring the PR targets the actual failing path in production.

examples.snippet: catch(Exception) { return OK; } // hides production error
Fix root cause + regression test + Sentry event link

items.title: Treat suspicious hardcoded tokens/keys as secrets and move them to a secret store
items.rule:
Never hardcode credentials (API keys, tokens, private keys) in source code, configs, or tests.
If a string looks like a credential based on context (variable name, header usage, auth flows), treat it as a secret even if it is not high-entropy. Move it to env/secret manager, rotate the credential if it may have leaked, and remove it from git history if necessary.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Hardcoded secrets are a common breach vector and often get copied into logs, client bundles, or forks.

examples.snippet: Read from env/secret manager; no secret literal in repo

items.title: Validate new/updated dependencies for CVEs and supply-chain risk
items.rule:
When a PR changes dependency manifests (e.g., package.json, yarn.lock, requirements.txt, composer.json), check for known vulnerabilities and risky packages.
- If an OSV/SCA MCP is available: query it for the new package versions and flag recent CVEs.
- Otherwise: require evidence (audit output, advisory links) in the PR description.
Also flag suspicious packages (typosquats, low-maintenance critical libs, unexpected transitive jumps) and require lockfile updates.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents supply-chain attacks and avoids introducing known-vulnerable versions.

examples.snippet: OSV/SCA result + pinned versions + updated lockfile

items.title: PR description must match code changes (no hidden scope creep)
items.rule:
Ensure the PR title/description honestly reflects what the diff actually changes.
Flag out-of-scope changes (behavioral changes hidden as refactors, unrelated dependency bumps, config/security changes) and require either:
- splitting into separate PRs, or
- an explicit 'Additional changes' section with rationale and risk/rollout notes.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents surprises, improves review quality, and reduces accidental risk in production.

examples.snippet: Description: "minor refactor"; diff changes access control
Description includes all risky changes + review guide

items.title: Warn when modifying unstable or high-latency endpoints
items.rule:
If a PR changes an API endpoint/controller/route, assess current production health before adding heavier logic.
- If a monitoring MCP is available (Datadog/Grafana): query current p95 latency and error rate for that endpoint.
- If the endpoint is already degraded, warn against adding heavy queries, blocking I/O, or complex synchronous logic; require performance evidence or a safe rollout plan.
To enable this check, the PR should reference the endpoint path (e.g., `POST /api/payments`) or the controller/action name.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Avoids worsening already-unstable services and reduces incident risk from incremental latency.

examples.snippet: Changed controller; no endpoint reference; no perf signal
Mentions route + includes p95/error snapshot + mitigation

items.title: Enforce architecture boundaries and layering rules
items.rule:
Do not violate the project's architecture boundaries.
Examples of violations: Controllers/Views directly querying the database; UI importing infrastructure modules; domain modules importing unrelated domains.
If the repo defines boundaries (lint rules, Nx tags, dependency-cruiser, ARCHITECTURE.md), enforce them. When in doubt, route all I/O through the intended service/repository layer and keep domain logic isolated.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Keeps modules decoupled, improves testability, and prevents long-term architectural erosion.

examples.snippet: controller.ts imports prisma/orm and queries DB

items.title: Deprecation guardian: prefer the new standard and follow the migration guide
items.rule:
If the PR introduces or expands usage of deprecated libraries/patterns the team is migrating away from (e.g., Moment.js, React Class Components), block and suggest the modern replacement.
If the repo includes a migration guide (e.g., `docs/migration.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `docs/adr/*`), follow it and propose the equivalent snippet.
If no guide exists, use an available web/search MCP to propose a safe, modern alternative and ask for confirmation of project standards.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents new tech debt and accelerates standardization across the codebase.

examples.snippet: Use approved date library + follow migration guide

items.title: Prefer the company design system over hand-rolled UI styles
items.rule:
Avoid re-creating existing UI components or duplicating styles (CSS/Tailwind) when the company design system already provides an official component.
If the PR touches UI and a design system entry point is referenced (e.g., `ui/index.ts`, `design-system.md`), use it as the source of truth and suggest the equivalent official component.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Improves visual consistency, reduces duplicate code, and speeds up future UI changes.

examples.snippet: New bespoke button/card styles duplicating DS

items.title: Keep API docs in sync with route/controller changes (OpenAPI/Swagger)
items.rule:
If a PR changes an API route/controller signature (path, method, params, request/response schema), update the API specification in the same PR.
To enable this check, reference the spec file path in the PR (e.g., `openapi.yaml`, `swagger.json`). If multiple specs exist, update the relevant one.
Warn when code and spec drift (new params not documented, status codes changed, response shape changed).
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents client breakage, reduces support load, and keeps contracts reliable.

items.title: Validate edge/CDN redirect and header rules against Cloudflare
items.rule:
If a PR changes redirect/header configuration (e.g., middleware redirects, CDN headers, security headers), validate it against the active edge rules to avoid conflicts.
- If a Cloudflare MCP is available: fetch active rules and detect potential redirect loops, duplicate rules, or header conflicts.
- Otherwise: require the author to link the relevant Cloudflare rules/screenshots in the PR description.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Edge misconfigurations can cause global outages (redirect loops) and security regressions.

examples.snippet: Cloudflare rule check + no loop + precedence documented

items.title: Block risky database migrations (locking ops, downtime risk)
items.rule:
Review SQL/database migrations for operations that can lock large tables or cause downtime.
Examples: creating indexes without CONCURRENTLY (Postgres), ALTER COLUMN TYPE on big tables, adding NOT NULL without backfill, long-running updates without batching.
Require an online migration strategy (CONCURRENTLY, backfill in batches, dual-write/expand-contract) and a rollback plan.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Unsafe migrations are a common cause of production outages and deploy rollbacks.

examples.snippet: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_x ON big_table(col);

items.title: Turn new TODOs into tracked tickets
items.rule:
If a PR adds new TODO/FIXME comments, require a tracking ticket.
Preferred formats: `TODO(ABC-123): ...` or `FIXME(ABC-123): ...`.
If a ticketing MCP is available, suggest (or create) the ticket and link it in the PR. Avoid leaving untracked TODOs in mainline code.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents technical debt from being forgotten and improves accountability.

examples.snippet: TODO(TICKET-ID) with link to backlog item

items.title: Check for active production incidents before merging
items.rule:
Before approving/merging risky changes, check whether there is an active critical production incident.
- If an incident/monitoring MCP is available (PagerDuty, Datadog Monitors): query for active Sev1/Sev2 incidents.
- If there is an active incident: warn the author and suggest delaying merges/deploys or using an explicit emergency override process.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Avoids piling changes onto an unstable system and reduces MTTR during incident response.

examples.snippet: Active incident exists; PR proceeds with no warning

items.title: Confirm Definition of Done (DoD) requirements from repo guidelines
items.rule:
If the repo defines a Definition of Done (e.g., `DOD.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`), ensure the PR meets it.
To enable this check, reference the file path in the PR or ask to review it explicitly. Confirm bureaucratic requirements (docs, tests, design approval, rollout plan) are satisfied or explicitly marked as not applicable.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Keeps releases consistent and reduces process gaps that lead to production issues.

examples.snippet: DoD exists but PR provides no evidence

items.title: Clean up feature flags when retiring code (avoid zombie flags)
items.rule:
When removing or decommissioning a feature, ensure the associated feature flag is also removed from:
- application code paths,
- config/registry files (e.g., flags.ts, config.json), and
- analytics/feature flag tools (if applicable).
If a PostHog (or similar) MCP is available, verify the flag is disabled/removed and not referenced elsewhere. Otherwise, require a repo-wide search evidence in the PR.
items.whyIsThisImportant: Prevents accumulating dead flags, reduces complexity, and avoids accidental re-enablement of retired code paths.

examples.snippet: Removed feature code; flag key still exists in flags.ts
